sourceNote: Original educational DSM-5-TR-aligned summary. This is not verbatim DSM text, not clinically validated, and not medical advice.

cases.title: Training Case 1
clues: A 39-year-old contractor is evaluated after repeated deceit, aggression, and legal problems during a student health appointment.
Consequences rarely change behavior.
There is repeated lying, impulsivity, irritability, reckless disregard, irresponsibility, or lack of remorse.
Work, family, and legal obligations are repeatedly disregarded.
Mania produces episodic recklessness rather than a stable pattern since youth.
The final clue is pervasive rights violation, deceit, impulsivity, irresponsibility, and lack of remorse.
cases.explanation: The case points to Antisocial Personality Disorder because there is a pervasive adult pattern of violating others' rights, with evidence of conduct problems before adulthood.

cases.title: Training Case 2
clues: A 22-year-old graduate student reports never feeling fully well between periods of worse depression during a telehealth follow-up.
Symptoms are less explosive than an acute episode but have become woven into daily life.
Friends say the mood problem predates the latest stressor by years.
The chronic mood state is accompanied by low appetite or overeating, poor sleep, fatigue, low self-esteem, and impaired concentration.
The core issue is duration and persistence of depressed mood.
The final clue is depressed mood most days across years, with low self-esteem, sleep problems, and hopelessness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 3
clues: A 41-year-old accountant reports low energy and trouble keeping up with usual responsibilities during a telehealth follow-up.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
The presentation is episodic and represents a clear change from previous functioning.
Family notes slowed speech, diminished pleasure, and difficulty making decisions.
The episode is more time-limited and symptom-dense than a lifelong low mood pattern.
The best-fitting diagnosis centers on a major depressive episode with distress or impairment and no manic history.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 4
clues: A 26-year-old medical student reports repetitive checking, washing, or mental rituals during a primary care consultation.
There is no substance or psychotic disorder that better explains the repetitive behavior.
The pattern consumes significant time and interferes with school, work, or relationships.
Compulsions include checking, washing, counting, ordering, reassurance-seeking, or mental reviewing.
The time-consuming ritual cycle is the diagnostic center.
The final clue is unwanted intrusive thoughts and repetitive rituals aimed at neutralizing anxiety.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 5
clues: A 29-year-old bartender continues drinking despite arguments, blackouts, and medical concerns during a primary care consultation.
Important activities have been reduced or abandoned.
Tolerance or withdrawal symptoms may appear.
Morning tremor or sweating improves after alcohol.
Depression or anxiety may coexist but does not explain the impaired control over alcohol.
The final clue is continued alcohol use despite harm plus tolerance or withdrawal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 6
clues: A 51-year-old trainer spends hours focused on a perceived flaw others barely notice during a student health appointment.
The concern is not primarily about body weight or fatness.
Work, school, dating, or leaving home is impaired.
Social avoidance comes from fear that others will notice the perceived defect.
Eating disorders center on weight or shape concerns with eating behavior disturbance.
The best-fitting diagnosis is an appearance preoccupation with repetitive behaviors and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 7
clues: A 9-year-old child reports unfinished tasks, missed deadlines, and difficulty sustaining attention during an outpatient visit.
Hyperactivity or impulsivity includes fidgeting, interrupting, impatience, and feeling driven.
Teachers, family, or coworkers describe a consistent pattern rather than a recent decline.
The person struggles with sustained effort even for important tasks.
Depression causes low motivation and slowed thinking during mood episodes.
The final clue is developmentally persistent attention and impulse-control symptoms with impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 8
clues: A 16-year-old student avoids situations where others might notice embarrassment during an outpatient visit.
The fear is not limited to body image concerns, trauma reminders, or autism-related social communication differences.
The person worries about blushing, shaking, sounding foolish, or being rejected.
The person uses safety behaviors such as rehearsing, avoiding eye contact, or speaking as little as possible.
Symptoms are not simply introversion because they cause distress and impairment.
The final clue is anxiety about scrutiny, embarrassment, and negative judgment across social situations.
cases.explanation: The case points to Social Anxiety Disorder because anxiety and avoidance center on possible negative evaluation in social or performance situations, causing impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 9
clues: A 9-year-old child reports unfinished tasks, missed deadlines, and difficulty sustaining attention during an emergency assessment.
The symptoms cause school, work, relationship, or safety impairment.
Inattention includes losing items, careless mistakes, distractibility, and difficulty finishing tasks.
Problems are developmental rather than new-onset in adulthood.
Autism includes social communication and restricted behavior patterns beyond attention symptoms.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a childhood-onset pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity across settings.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 10
clues: A 64-year-old retiree repeatedly checks the body and searches online for disease signs during a primary care consultation.
The preoccupation has lasted months and reassurance fades quickly.
Medical evaluation has not supported the feared illness.
Functional impairment comes from disease preoccupation.
Somatic Symptom Disorder has prominent distressing symptoms.
The final clue is disease preoccupation, checking, and reassurance cycles despite little somatic burden.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 11
clues: A 28-year-old chef describes years of alternating emotional highs and lows that never fully meet episode thresholds during an outpatient visit.
The pattern has lasted for years with many periods of hypomanic symptoms and depressive symptoms.
Substance use and medical causes do not explain the pattern.
At times the person sleeps less, talks more, and takes on projects; at other times motivation and self-esteem drop.
A full hypomanic episode plus major depression would indicate Bipolar II Disorder.
The case is tougher because it sits below the threshold for Bipolar I or II.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 12
clues: A 36-year-old parent feels shame after secret eating episodes but does not purge during a primary care consultation.
Weight can vary and is not the diagnostic requirement.
Binges occur with rapid eating, eating when not hungry, eating until uncomfortably full, or eating alone from embarrassment.
Distress about binge eating is clinically significant.
Night Eating Syndrome has a circadian eating pattern rather than discrete loss-of-control binges.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent distressing binge eating without compensatory behavior.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 13
clues: A 42-year-old project manager reports unfinished tasks, missed deadlines, and difficulty sustaining attention during a student health appointment.
Mood, anxiety, sleep, substance, and learning problems do not fully explain the pattern.
The symptoms cause school, work, relationship, or safety impairment.
External structure helps but does not eliminate the symptoms.
Anxiety can impair concentration but is driven by worry.
The final clue is developmentally persistent attention and impulse-control symptoms with impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 14
clues: A 30-year-old delivery driver uses cocaine or methamphetamine despite paranoia, insomnia, and missed obligations during a telehealth follow-up.
Tolerance, risky use, and continued use despite harm may be present.
Sleep and appetite are disrupted.
Use continues despite medical danger or psychotic symptoms.
Bipolar mania can look similar but is not tied to stimulant intoxication and withdrawal cycles.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic stimulant use pattern with craving, impairment, risky use, and continued use despite harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 15
clues: A 20-year-old student describes years of alternating emotional highs and lows that never fully meet episode thresholds during a family-requested evaluation.
The pattern has lasted for years with many periods of hypomanic symptoms and depressive symptoms.
There are no psychotic symptoms and no history of hospitalization for mood elevation.
At times the person sleeps less, talks more, and takes on projects; at other times motivation and self-esteem drop.
A full manic episode would indicate Bipolar I Disorder.
The final clue is years of alternating hypomanic and depressive symptoms without full manic, hypomanic, or major depressive episodes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 16
clues: A 18-year-old student says the mind rarely turns off even when problems are minor during a family-requested evaluation.
Medical causes and stimulant use have been considered.
The worry has been present for months and spans health, money, work, family, and small decisions.
Avoidance and reassurance-seeking are spreading across daily life.
The breadth of worry distinguishes it from phobias and social anxiety.
The best-fitting diagnosis is chronic excessive worry across many domains with physical tension and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 17
clues: A 28-year-old artist reports chronic emptiness, abandonment panic, and impulsive crises during a family-requested evaluation.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
The pattern is long-standing and appears across relationships and settings.
Relationships alternate between idealization and devaluation.
Bipolar II mood episodes last days and include increased energy, not only interpersonal reactivity.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a pervasive pattern of instability in relationships, self-image, affect, and impulse control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 18
clues: A 36-year-old engineer says the mind rarely turns off even when problems are minor during a telehealth follow-up.
The anxiety is not limited to scrutiny, contamination, trauma reminders, or a single phobic object.
Reassurance helps briefly but the worry quickly shifts to another topic.
The person describes excessive worry that is hard to control.
The time course distinguishes it from brief stress reactions.
The final clue is difficult-to-control worry more days than not, across multiple topics.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 19
clues: A 47-year-old office worker spends hours monitoring bodily sensations and seeking reassurance during a primary care consultation.
Anxiety, depression, and medical illness may coexist.
Time, energy, and emotion devoted to symptoms are high.
Reassurance is short-lived.
Illness Anxiety Disorder has minimal or no somatic symptoms.
The final clue is disproportionate health-related preoccupation around actual symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Somatic Symptom Disorder because distressing physical symptoms are accompanied by excessive health-related thoughts, anxiety, and behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 20
clues: A 45-year-old parent describes cycles of bingeing and vomiting while weight remains not significantly low during a primary care consultation.
Binge episodes involve loss of control and unusually large intake.
Medical complications such as electrolyte problems or parotid swelling may appear.
The person feels unable to control the binge-purge cycle.
Binge-Eating Disorder lacks recurrent compensatory behaviors.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent binge eating with compensatory behaviors and shape-weight overvaluation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bulimia Nervosa because recurrent binge eating with loss of control is followed by compensatory behaviors, with self-evaluation tied to weight or shape.

cases.title: Training Case 21
clues: A 47-year-old mechanic reports voices and paranoia along with episodes of mania or depression during a telehealth follow-up.
Major mood episodes are also present for a large portion of the illness.
Substance use does not explain the psychotic course.
Functional decline and disorganization may appear during psychotic periods.
Mood disorder with psychotic features has psychosis only during mood episodes.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 22
clues: A 29-year-old teacher is brought in after weeks of social withdrawal and missed obligations during a student health appointment.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
Sleep, appetite, concentration, and self-worth have changed in the same period.
Most days include depressed mood or marked loss of interest, plus guilt, fatigue, and poor concentration.
The key clinical weight is an affective episode with neurovegetative and cognitive changes.
The syndrome is best understood as a unipolar depressive episode rather than a primary anxiety, trauma, or psychotic disorder.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 23
clues: A 39-year-old parent is evaluated after recent onset of disorganized speech and suspicious beliefs during an outpatient visit.
Function may be impaired, but the course has not yet reached the chronic duration threshold.
Family reports the onset was noticeable and relatively recent.
Symptoms persist beyond a brief psychotic episode.
Schizophrenia requires a longer illness duration.
The best-fitting diagnosis is schizophrenia-like psychosis in the one-to-six-month duration window.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophreniform Disorder because schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms have lasted longer than brief psychosis but have not reached the longer schizophrenia duration threshold.

cases.title: Training Case 24
clues: A 25-year-old software tester reports repeated abrupt surges of fear that peak within minutes during a telehealth follow-up.
The fear is of the panic sensations and consequences, not one object or social judgment.
For weeks afterward the person worries about more attacks and changes behavior.
Anticipatory anxiety about future attacks has become disabling.
If attacks occur only with one phobic cue, a phobia may be primary.
The case is not simply stress because the attacks are abrupt, recurrent, and followed by avoidance.
cases.explanation: The case points to Panic Disorder because recurrent unexpected panic attacks are followed by persistent concern about attacks and avoidance of internal cues or situations.

cases.title: Training Case 25
clues: A 63-year-old retiree has daytime fatigue and irritability despite adequate chance to sleep during a family-requested evaluation.
Caffeine, shift work, substances, and untreated sleep apnea are assessed.
The problem persists despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep.
Poor sleep becomes a conditioned cycle.
Circadian rhythm disorders involve misalignment of sleep timing.
The case favors Insomnia Disorder because sleep difficulty itself is the central complaint.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 26
clues: A 41-year-old club promoter reports binges of stimulant use followed by crashes and failed attempts to stop during an emergency assessment.
Financial, legal, occupational, or interpersonal consequences are mounting.
Tolerance, risky use, and continued use despite harm may be present.
Binges lead to days without sleep and then exhaustion.
Primary psychotic disorder persists beyond substance effects.
The final clue is stimulant binges with insomnia, crash, paranoia, and loss of control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 27
clues: A 41-year-old accountant reports low energy and trouble keeping up with usual responsibilities during an emergency assessment.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
The presentation is episodic and represents a clear change from previous functioning.
The person reports feeling worthless and has passive thoughts that life is not worth much, without psychotic symptoms.
The episode is more time-limited and symptom-dense than a lifelong low mood pattern.
The final clue is a sustained depressive episode with impaired functioning and no substance or bipolar explanation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 28
clues: A 52-year-old office worker holds a fixed belief that is not shared by others but otherwise appears organized during a student health appointment.
Hallucinations, if present, are not prominent and relate to the delusional theme.
Mood episodes are absent or brief relative to the delusional period.
The presentation is circumscribed compared with schizophrenia.
Schizophrenia has broader psychosis and functional decline.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a persistent delusion with otherwise relatively preserved functioning.
cases.explanation: The case points to Delusional Disorder because a fixed delusional belief persists while broader schizophrenia-spectrum features and marked functional decline are absent.

cases.title: Training Case 29
clues: A 49-year-old attorney reports persistent difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking too early during a family-requested evaluation.
Anxiety or depression may coexist but does not fully explain the sleep complaint.
The problem persists despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep.
Poor sleep becomes a conditioned cycle.
Circadian rhythm disorders involve misalignment of sleep timing.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty with daytime impairment despite adequate sleep opportunity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 30
clues: A 8-year-old child loses weight after narrowing food intake without body image concerns during a primary care consultation.
The person may avoid textures, smells, swallowing sensations, or fear vomiting.
There is no intense fear of weight gain or disturbance in body image.
The avoidance is not motivated by pursuit of thinness.
Anorexia Nervosa has weight-gain fear or body image disturbance.
The best-fitting diagnosis is restrictive intake with nutritional or functional consequences and no body image driver.
cases.explanation: The case points to Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder because restrictive intake causes nutritional or psychosocial impairment without weight or shape disturbance.

cases.title: Training Case 31
clues: A 25-year-old artist has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during an emergency assessment.
There has been a period of psychosis lasting at least two weeks without a major mood episode.
Substance use does not explain the psychotic course.
Mood episodes are prominent across the overall illness.
Mood disorder with psychotic features has psychosis only during mood episodes.
The best-fitting diagnosis is psychosis outside mood episodes plus mood episodes present for much of the illness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 32
clues: A 35-year-old parent is preoccupied with cancer, neurologic disease, or heart disease despite reassurance during a telehealth follow-up.
The fear is disease-focused, not a panic surge or broad everyday worry.
Somatic symptoms are absent or mild.
Health-related checking, reassurance-seeking, or avoidance is prominent.
Generalized Anxiety Disorder includes many worry domains rather than illness preoccupation.
The final clue is disease preoccupation, checking, and reassurance cycles despite little somatic burden.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 33
clues: A 47-year-old sibling continues to feel life is frozen after the death of a loved one during a telehealth follow-up.
The person avoids reminders yet also searches for closeness to the deceased.
The dominant emotion is yearning, longing, or preoccupation rather than generalized low mood alone.
The person denies a wish to die but says life feels empty without the deceased.
Major depression is broader and not necessarily centered on separation distress.
The final clue is prolonged separation distress, identity disruption, and functional impairment after bereavement.
cases.explanation: The case points to Prolonged Grief Disorder because persistent yearning and preoccupation after bereavement remain intense, impairing, and grief-specific well beyond the acute mourning period.

cases.title: Training Case 34
clues: A 19-year-old college student is seen after marked weight loss and rigid food rules during a telehealth follow-up.
The person evaluates self-worth heavily through shape, weight, or control.
Binge-purge behavior may or may not be present.
Body image is distorted or weight's seriousness is minimized.
Bulimia Nervosa usually has normal or higher weight and recurrent binge-purge cycles.
The case favors Anorexia Nervosa because restriction and low weight are central.
cases.explanation: The case points to Anorexia Nervosa because restriction leads to significantly low weight, with fear of weight gain and disturbed body image or denial of seriousness.

cases.title: Training Case 35
clues: A 28-year-old chef describes years of alternating emotional highs and lows that never fully meet episode thresholds during a student health appointment.
The pattern has lasted for years with many periods of hypomanic symptoms and depressive symptoms.
The person says the shifts are more chronic than episodic major depression.
The highs and lows are subthreshold but recurrent.
A full hypomanic episode plus major depression would indicate Bipolar II Disorder.
The final clue is years of alternating hypomanic and depressive symptoms without full manic, hypomanic, or major depressive episodes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 36
clues: A 67-year-old widower reports never feeling fully well between periods of worse depression during a primary care consultation.
The person continues working but describes constant low energy, poor sleep, and hopelessness.
Symptoms are less explosive than an acute episode but have become woven into daily life.
The mood problem is long-term rather than confined to a recent two-week episode.
Symptoms are not better explained by a personality disorder, substance use, or bipolar spectrum condition.
The case favors a persistent depressive pattern rather than a single isolated depressive episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 37
clues: A 48-year-old pharmacist describes intrusive thoughts that feel unwanted and distressing during a primary care consultation.
The thoughts are ego-dystonic or resisted rather than welcomed.
There is no substance or psychotic disorder that better explains the repetitive behavior.
Obsessions include contamination, harm, symmetry, taboo thoughts, or fear of mistakes.
The time-consuming ritual cycle is the diagnostic center.
The case favors OCD rather than generalized worry because of the ritualized neutralizing behaviors.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 38
clues: A 45-year-old consultant seeks help because mood instability has become a long-term pattern during a student health appointment.
Neither the high nor low periods clearly meet full criteria for hypomanic, manic, or major depressive episodes.
The person says the shifts are more chronic than episodic major depression.
The highs and lows are subthreshold but recurrent.
A full hypomanic episode plus major depression would indicate Bipolar II Disorder.
The final clue is years of alternating hypomanic and depressive symptoms without full manic, hypomanic, or major depressive episodes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 39
clues: A 30-year-old delivery driver continues stimulant use despite chest pain, job loss, and relationship conflict during a primary care consultation.
Use occurs in larger amounts or longer runs than intended.
The person may have stimulant-induced paranoia, tactile sensations, or mood crashes.
Use continues despite medical danger or psychotic symptoms.
Bipolar mania can look similar but is not tied to stimulant intoxication and withdrawal cycles.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic stimulant use pattern with craving, impairment, risky use, and continued use despite harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 40
clues: A 65-year-old retiree has a persistent persecutory, jealous, grandiose, somatic, or erotomanic belief during a family-requested evaluation.
Mood episodes are absent or brief relative to the delusional period.
Hallucinations, if present, are not prominent and relate to the delusional theme.
The belief is fixed despite contrary evidence.
OCD can have poor insight but thoughts are often intrusive and ritual-linked.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a persistent delusion with otherwise relatively preserved functioning.
cases.explanation: The case points to Delusional Disorder because a fixed delusional belief persists while broader schizophrenia-spectrum features and marked functional decline are absent.

cases.title: Training Case 41
clues: A 41-year-old club promoter uses cocaine or methamphetamine despite paranoia, insomnia, and missed obligations during an outpatient visit.
Use occurs in larger amounts or longer runs than intended.
Financial, legal, occupational, or interpersonal consequences are mounting.
Attempts to quit trigger dysphoria, fatigue, and craving.
Primary psychotic disorder persists beyond substance effects.
The final clue is stimulant binges with insomnia, crash, paranoia, and loss of control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 42
clues: A 36-year-old parent has recurrent binge episodes associated with distress during an outpatient visit.
The person describes loss of control rather than ordinary overeating.
The person feels disgusted, depressed, or guilty afterward.
The binge episodes are recurrent over time.
Bulimia Nervosa includes recurrent compensatory behaviors.
The final clue is loss-of-control binges with shame and no regular purging or fasting.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 43
clues: A 28-year-old artist presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during a primary care consultation.
Mood shifts are rapid and reactive, often lasting hours rather than discrete mood episodes.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
Anger, emptiness, and identity disturbance are prominent.
Histrionic Personality Disorder is attention-seeking but usually lacks self-harm and abandonment panic.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a pervasive pattern of instability in relationships, self-image, affect, and impulse control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 44
clues: A 63-year-old retiree dreads bedtime because sleep has become unpredictable during a family-requested evaluation.
The problem persists despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep.
Anxiety or depression may coexist but does not fully explain the sleep complaint.
Poor sleep becomes a conditioned cycle.
Sleep apnea includes breathing disturbance and nonrestorative sleep.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty with daytime impairment despite adequate sleep opportunity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 45
clues: A 12-year-old child avoids many foods after choking, nausea, or sensory distress during a family-requested evaluation.
Mealtimes create family stress and social avoidance.
The pattern is more than ordinary picky eating.
Food variety is very limited and health or functioning is affected.
Autism may coexist, but ARFID describes the feeding disturbance itself.
The final clue is food restriction from sensory or fear-based avoidance without weight-shape overvaluation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder because restrictive intake causes nutritional or psychosocial impairment without weight or shape disturbance.

cases.title: Training Case 46
clues: A 61-year-old retiree keeps newspapers, containers, clothing, and broken objects for possible future use during an outpatient visit.
The difficulty discarding has grown over years.
Fire safety, falls, or family conflict have become concerns.
The person resists cleanup because each item feels potentially important.
Collectors organize and display items; hoarding creates clutter and impairment.
The case favors Hoarding Disorder because clutter and saving are the central syndrome.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 47
clues: A 41-year-old parent presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during a family-requested evaluation.
Mood shifts are rapid and reactive, often lasting hours rather than discrete mood episodes.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
Relationships alternate between idealization and devaluation.
Bipolar II mood episodes last days and include increased energy, not only interpersonal reactivity.
The case favors Borderline Personality Disorder because the pattern is trait-like and interpersonal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 48
clues: A 26-year-old medical student arrives late because rituals take hours each morning during an emergency assessment.
The concern is not limited to appearance, hoarding, illness anxiety, or ordinary worry.
The rituals temporarily reduce anxiety but the relief fades quickly.
Avoidance grows around triggers that set off obsessions.
Delusions are fixed beliefs; OCD thoughts are intrusive and repetitive even when insight is poor.
The best-fitting diagnosis is obsessions plus compulsions that are distressing, time-consuming, or impairing.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 49
clues: A 51-year-old veteran continues opioid use despite overdoses, cravings, and failed attempts to stop during a primary care consultation.
Use has escalated and control has diminished.
Craving remains strong despite serious consequences.
Withdrawal includes aches, diarrhea, rhinorrhea, insomnia, anxiety, and cravings.
Sedative or alcohol use disorder may coexist and increases overdose risk.
The case favors Opioid Use Disorder because opioid-related impairment and loss of control are central.
cases.explanation: The case points to Opioid Use Disorder because opioid use is marked by impaired control, craving, role impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and withdrawal or tolerance features.

cases.title: Training Case 50
clues: A 28-year-old nurse dreads bedtime because sleep has become unpredictable during a primary care consultation.
The sleep difficulty occurs repeatedly over months.
The person spends increasing time in bed trying to force sleep.

cases.title: Training Case 151
clues: A 55-year-old teacher feels intense distress when family tries to discard possessions during a student health appointment.

cases.title: Training Case 152

cases.title: Training Case 153
clues: A 19-year-old college student continues restriction and exercise even when dizzy and cold during an outpatient visit.

Body image is distorted or weight's seriousness is minimized.

cases.title: Training Case 154

cases.title: Training Case 156
clues: A 52-year-old office worker holds a fixed belief that is not shared by others but otherwise appears organized during a telehealth follow-up.

clues: A 22-year-old graduate student presents with chronic pessimism, fatigue, and low self-confidence during a family-requested evaluation.
Symptoms are less explosive than an acute episode but have become woven into daily life.
The person continues working but describes constant low energy, poor sleep, and hopelessness.
The chronic mood state is accompanied by low appetite or overeating, poor sleep, fatigue, low self-esteem, and impaired concentration.
Symptoms are not better explained by a personality disorder, substance use, or bipolar spectrum condition.
The case favors a persistent depressive pattern rather than a single isolated depressive episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 402
clues: A 47-year-old office worker spends hours monitoring bodily sensations and seeking reassurance during a student health appointment.
The person repeatedly researches symptoms and seeks consultations.
The symptoms are not intentionally produced.
Reassurance is short-lived.
Illness Anxiety Disorder has minimal or no somatic symptoms.
The case favors Somatic Symptom Disorder because symptoms themselves are central and impairing.
cases.explanation: The case points to Somatic Symptom Disorder because distressing physical symptoms are accompanied by excessive health-related thoughts, anxiety, and behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 403
clues: A 36-year-old parent has recurrent binge episodes associated with distress during an outpatient visit.

Binges occur with rapid eating, eating when not hungry, eating until uncomfortably full, or eating alone from embarrassment.
The absence of compensatory behavior separates it from bulimia.
Bulimia Nervosa includes recurrent compensatory behaviors.
The final clue is loss-of-control binges with shame and no regular purging or fasting.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 404
clues: A 15-year-old student is referred for chronic disorganization, forgetfulness, and restlessness during a family-requested evaluation.
Hyperactivity or impulsivity includes fidgeting, interrupting, impatience, and feeling driven.
The symptoms cause school, work, relationship, or safety impairment.
Problems are developmental rather than new-onset in adulthood.
Anxiety can impair concentration but is driven by worry.
The case favors ADHD because symptoms are chronic, cross-situational, and began early.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 405
clues: A 15-year-old student is referred for chronic disorganization, forgetfulness, and restlessness during a student health appointment.
Hyperactivity or impulsivity includes fidgeting, interrupting, impatience, and feeling driven.
Teachers, family, or coworkers describe a consistent pattern rather than a recent decline.
The person struggles with sustained effort even for important tasks.

cases.title: Training Case 406
clues: A 63-year-old retired nurse describes feeling slowed down and unable to enjoy previous routines during an emergency assessment.
Symptoms have clustered for more than two weeks rather than appearing as brief mood shifts.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
Most days include depressed mood or marked loss of interest, plus guilt, fatigue, and poor concentration.
Symptoms are not limited to anxiety about one topic, traumatic reminders, or chronic personality traits.
The syndrome is best understood as a unipolar depressive episode rather than a primary anxiety, trauma, or psychotic disorder.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 407
clues: A 7-year-old student reports lifelong difficulty reading social cues alongside unusually focused interests during a student health appointment.
The presentation is not explained only by social fear or attention problems.
Social reciprocity, nonverbal communication, and peer relationships are affected.

The best-fitting diagnosis is early-emerging social communication differences plus restricted or repetitive patterns.
cases.explanation: The case points to Autism Spectrum Disorder because persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors are present from early development and cause functional impact.

cases.title: Training Case 408
clues: A 31-year-old nurse has persistent physical symptoms with disproportionate health-related anxiety during a primary care consultation.
The person repeatedly researches symptoms and seeks consultations.
The symptoms are not intentionally produced.

cases.explanation: The case points to Somatic Symptom Disorder because distressing physical symptoms are accompanied by excessive health-related thoughts, anxiety, and behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 409
clues: A 63-year-old retired nurse describes feeling slowed down and unable to enjoy previous routines during a primary care consultation.
The presentation is episodic and represents a clear change from previous functioning.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.

The key clinical weight is an affective episode with neurovegetative and cognitive changes.
The best-fitting diagnosis centers on a major depressive episode with distress or impairment and no manic history.

cases.title: Training Case 410
clues: A 45-year-old consultant describes years of alternating emotional highs and lows that never fully meet episode thresholds during an emergency assessment.

cases.title: Training Case 411
clues: A 42-year-old project manager reports unfinished tasks, missed deadlines, and difficulty sustaining attention during an outpatient visit.
Mood, anxiety, sleep, substance, and learning problems do not fully explain the pattern.
Symptoms began in childhood and appear in more than one setting.
The person struggles with sustained effort even for important tasks.
Anxiety can impair concentration but is driven by worry.
The final clue is developmentally persistent attention and impulse-control symptoms with impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 412
clues: A 35-year-old parent fears having a serious illness despite minimal symptoms during a family-requested evaluation.
The concern is not better explained by delusional disorder or OCD.

The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent fear of having or acquiring serious illness with minimal symptoms.

cases.title: Training Case 413
clues: A 51-year-old veteran uses more pills or heroin than intended and spends the day obtaining or recovering during a primary care consultation.

cases.title: Training Case 414
clues: A 30-year-old delivery driver continues stimulant use despite chest pain, job loss, and relationship conflict during a family-requested evaluation.

The case favors Stimulant Use Disorder because the symptoms track stimulant use and consequences.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 415
clues: A 41-year-old accountant is brought in after weeks of social withdrawal and missed obligations during a primary care consultation.

There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
The person reports feeling worthless and has passive thoughts that life is not worth much, without psychotic symptoms.
The key clinical weight is an affective episode with neurovegetative and cognitive changes.
The best-fitting diagnosis centers on a major depressive episode with distress or impairment and no manic history.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 416
clues: A 31-year-old entrepreneur has become unusually energetic, irritable, and hard to interrupt during a student health appointment.

cases.title: Training Case 417
clues: A 36-year-old parent continues to feel life is frozen after the death of a loved one during a telehealth follow-up.

cases.title: Training Case 418
clues: A 26-year-old medical student reports repetitive checking, washing, or mental rituals during an emergency assessment.

cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 419

The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic opioid use pattern with impaired control, harm, craving, and possible withdrawal.

The case favors Schizophrenia because duration, breadth, and decline exceed brief or mood-related psychosis.

cases.title: Training Case 422
clues: A 67-year-old retiree has tried to cut down drinking but repeatedly returns to heavy use during a primary care consultation.

The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic pattern of alcohol use with impaired control, impairment, risky use, or pharmacologic features.

cases.title: Training Case 423
clues: A 44-year-old surgeon misses obligations and hides bottles despite consequences during a telehealth follow-up.
Important activities have been reduced or abandoned.
Craving, time spent drinking or recovering, and role impairment are present.
Morning tremor or sweating improves after alcohol.
Depression or anxiety may coexist but does not explain the impaired control over alcohol.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic pattern of alcohol use with impaired control, impairment, risky use, or pharmacologic features.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 424
clues: A 31-year-old attorney has dental enamel erosion, shame, and repeated attempts to stop purging during an emergency assessment.

Binge-Eating Disorder lacks recurrent compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 425
clues: A 27-year-old dancer reports intense fear of weight gain despite being medically underweight during an outpatient visit.
The problem is not simply low appetite from depression or fear of choking.
The person evaluates self-worth heavily through shape, weight, or control.

cases.title: Training Case 426
clues: A 61-year-old retiree keeps newspapers, containers, clothing, and broken objects for possible future use during an outpatient visit.
Acquiring items may be excessive but the key problem is discarding.

The case favors Hoarding Disorder because clutter and saving are the central syndrome.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 427

Nightmares, flashbacks, or unwanted memories recur despite efforts to avoid them.

cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 428

Financial, legal, occupational, or interpersonal consequences are mounting.

ADHD treatment taken as prescribed is not stimulant use disorder.
The final clue is stimulant binges with insomnia, crash, paranoia, and loss of control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 429
clues: A 54-year-old store manager reports never feeling fully well between periods of worse depression during a student health appointment.
There have not been symptom-free stretches longer than a couple of months.
The person continues working but describes constant low energy, poor sleep, and hopelessness.
The mood problem is long-term rather than confined to a recent two-week episode.

The final clue is depressed mood most days across years, with low self-esteem, sleep problems, and hopelessness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 430
clues: A 31-year-old attorney has dental enamel erosion, shame, and repeated attempts to stop purging during a family-requested evaluation.
Binge episodes involve loss of control and unusually large intake.
The pattern recurs over time and is not limited to episodes of anorexia nervosa.
The person feels unable to control the binge-purge cycle.

cases.title: Training Case 431

cases.title: Training Case 432

cases.title: Training Case 433

cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 435
clues: A 49-year-old sales lead presents with a current depressive episode but mentions bursts of unusually high productivity during an emergency assessment.

The hypomanic periods are clear departures from baseline but do not become full mania.

cases.title: Training Case 436

The case favors Autism Spectrum Disorder because both social communication and restricted behavior domains are present.
cases.explanation: The case points to Autism Spectrum Disorder because persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors are present from early development and cause functional impact.

cases.title: Training Case 437

cases.title: Training Case 438

Sleep and appetite are disrupted.

cases.title: Training Case 443
clues: A 22-year-old college student reports hearing voices while self-care and work have deteriorated during a primary care consultation.
Negative symptoms such as reduced speech, low motivation, or flat affect are present.
Hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech, or disorganized behavior are prominent.
Family reports deterioration in hygiene, social engagement, and goal-directed behavior.
Schizoaffective Disorder requires a substantial mood component plus psychosis outside mood episodes.
The case favors Schizophrenia because duration, breadth, and decline exceed brief or mood-related psychosis.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophrenia because psychotic symptoms, negative symptoms, functional decline, and a prolonged course occur outside mood episodes and without a better substance or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 444
clues: A 34-year-old warehouse worker is brought in after months of odd beliefs and functional decline during an outpatient visit.
Negative symptoms such as reduced speech, low motivation, or flat affect are present.
Mood episodes are absent or occupy a minority of the illness.

Delusional Disorder lacks the broader deterioration and hallucination/disorganization pattern.
