cases.title: Training Case 450
clues: A 51-year-old trainer avoids mirrors and photos because of distress about appearance during an outpatient visit.
Work, school, dating, or leaving home is impaired.
Medical examination does not match the intensity of the concern.
Social avoidance comes from fear that others will notice the perceived defect.
OCD can include appearance obsessions, but BDD is specifically about perceived defects.
The case favors Body Dysmorphic Disorder rather than social anxiety because appearance preoccupation drives the avoidance.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 451
clues: A 47-year-old mechanic has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during a primary care consultation.
Major mood episodes are also present for a large portion of the illness.
During mood episodes, psychosis intensifies, but it does not disappear completely between them.
Functional decline and disorganization may appear during psychotic periods.
Bipolar I with psychotic features does not have extended psychosis outside mania or depression.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 452
clues: A 30-year-old delivery driver continues stimulant use despite chest pain, job loss, and relationship conflict during a student health appointment.
Tolerance, risky use, and continued use despite harm may be present.
Sleep and appetite are disrupted.
Binges lead to days without sleep and then exhaustion.
Bipolar mania can look similar but is not tied to stimulant intoxication and withdrawal cycles.
The final clue is stimulant binges with insomnia, crash, paranoia, and loss of control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 453
clues: A 32-year-old man has a long pattern of violating others' rights and disregarding consequences during an emergency assessment.
Consequences rarely change behavior.
The person is at least an adult and the behavior is not limited to mania or psychosis.
Risk-taking continues despite danger to self or others.
Mania produces episodic recklessness rather than a stable pattern since youth.
The case favors Antisocial Personality Disorder because the pattern is developmental and persistent.
cases.explanation: The case points to Antisocial Personality Disorder because there is a pervasive adult pattern of violating others' rights, with evidence of conduct problems before adulthood.

cases.title: Training Case 454
clues: A 29-year-old collector is referred after rooms became unusable because of saved items during an outpatient visit.
The clutter is not better explained by low energy during depression or delusional infestation beliefs.
The difficulty discarding has grown over years.
The person resists cleanup because each item feels potentially important.
OCD rituals may involve saving, but hoarding disorder is defined by discarding difficulty.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent difficulty discarding possessions with clutter and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 455
clues: A 46-year-old parent is brought in after several nights with almost no sleep and escalating plans during an outpatient visit.
Psychotic intensity or hospitalization has become part of the episode.
The episode is clearly different from the person's baseline.
Irritability escalates when others challenge grand plans.
A major depressive episode is common but is not required for the diagnosis.
The episode crosses the threshold from hypomania into mania.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar I Disorder because it describes a manic episode with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, risky behavior, and marked impairment or psychosis.

cases.title: Training Case 456
clues: A 46-year-old parent is brought in after several nights with almost no sleep and escalating plans during a telehealth follow-up.
Psychotic intensity or hospitalization has become part of the episode.
The episode is clearly different from the person's baseline.
Ideas shift quickly, speech is pressured, and judgment is markedly impaired.
A major depressive episode is common but is not required for the diagnosis.
The best-fitting diagnosis requires at least one manic episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar I Disorder because it describes a manic episode with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, risky behavior, and marked impairment or psychosis.

cases.title: Training Case 457
clues: A 28-year-old nurse reports persistent difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking too early during an emergency assessment.
The sleep difficulty occurs repeatedly over months.
The person spends increasing time in bed trying to force sleep.
Poor sleep becomes a conditioned cycle.
Circadian rhythm disorders involve misalignment of sleep timing.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty with daytime impairment despite adequate sleep opportunity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 458
clues: A 30-year-old lab technician develops psychotic symptoms over several weeks with uncertain long-term course during an outpatient visit.
The disturbance has lasted more than a month but less than six months.
Mood episodes do not dominate the illness.
Symptoms persist beyond a brief psychotic episode.
Schizophrenia requires a longer illness duration.
The case is a duration puzzle: schizophrenia-spectrum symptoms without schizophrenia duration.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophreniform Disorder because schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms have lasted longer than brief psychosis but have not reached the longer schizophrenia duration threshold.

cases.title: Training Case 459
clues: A 19-year-old college student is seen after marked weight loss and rigid food rules during a telehealth follow-up.
Binge-purge behavior may or may not be present.
The person evaluates self-worth heavily through shape, weight, or control.
Body image is distorted or weight's seriousness is minimized.
Bulimia Nervosa usually has normal or higher weight and recurrent binge-purge cycles.
The case favors Anorexia Nervosa because restriction and low weight are central.
cases.explanation: The case points to Anorexia Nervosa because restriction leads to significantly low weight, with fear of weight gain and disturbed body image or denial of seriousness.

cases.title: Training Case 460
clues: A 16-year-old athlete continues restriction and exercise even when dizzy and cold during an emergency assessment.
Body weight is significantly low for age, sex, development, and health context.
Binge-purge behavior may or may not be present.
Fear of gaining weight persists despite medical risk.
ARFID lacks fear of weight gain or body image disturbance.
The final clue is low weight plus persistent weight-gain fear and disturbed body experience.
cases.explanation: The case points to Anorexia Nervosa because restriction leads to significantly low weight, with fear of weight gain and disturbed body image or denial of seriousness.

cases.title: Training Case 461
clues: A 27-year-old dancer is seen after marked weight loss and rigid food rules during a telehealth follow-up.
The problem is not simply low appetite from depression or fear of choking.
Food intake is persistently restricted relative to needs.
Body image is distorted or weight's seriousness is minimized.
ARFID lacks fear of weight gain or body image disturbance.
The final clue is low weight plus persistent weight-gain fear and disturbed body experience.
cases.explanation: The case points to Anorexia Nervosa because restriction leads to significantly low weight, with fear of weight gain and disturbed body image or denial of seriousness.

cases.title: Training Case 462
clues: A 41-year-old parent describes intense relationships that swing between closeness and rupture during a telehealth follow-up.
Identity, goals, and self-image feel unstable.
Recurrent self-harm, suicidal gestures, or threats occur during crises.
Anger, emptiness, and identity disturbance are prominent.
PTSD is trauma-anchored with intrusion and avoidance clusters.
The final clue is abandonment fear, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, and self-harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 463
clues: A 34-year-old warehouse worker reports hearing voices while self-care and work have deteriorated during a primary care consultation.
Negative symptoms such as reduced speech, low motivation, or flat affect are present.
Mood episodes are absent or occupy a minority of the illness.
Family reports deterioration in hygiene, social engagement, and goal-directed behavior.
Delusional Disorder lacks the broader deterioration and hallucination/disorganization pattern.
The final clue is psychosis lasting months with deterioration and symptoms not restricted to mood episodes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophrenia because psychotic symptoms, negative symptoms, functional decline, and a prolonged course occur outside mood episodes and without a better substance or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 464
clues: A 39-year-old parent develops psychotic symptoms over several weeks with uncertain long-term course during an emergency assessment.
The disturbance has lasted more than a month but less than six months.
There is no clear substance, medication, or medical cause.
The person may be too early in the course to determine whether schizophrenia will develop.
Brief Psychotic Disorder lasts less than one month.
The final clue is active psychotic symptoms lasting more than a month but not yet six months.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophreniform Disorder because schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms have lasted longer than brief psychosis but have not reached the longer schizophrenia duration threshold.

cases.title: Training Case 465
clues: A 47-year-old office worker reports pain or fatigue that has taken over daily life during a student health appointment.
The person repeatedly researches symptoms and seeks consultations.
Anxiety, depression, and medical illness may coexist.
Functional impairment comes from health-related thoughts, feelings, and behaviors.
Factitious Disorder involves deception without obvious external rewards.
The final clue is disproportionate health-related preoccupation around actual symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Somatic Symptom Disorder because distressing physical symptoms are accompanied by excessive health-related thoughts, anxiety, and behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 466
clues: A 47-year-old mechanic reports voices and paranoia along with episodes of mania or depression during a student health appointment.
Substance use does not explain the psychotic course.
There has been a period of psychosis lasting at least two weeks without a major mood episode.
Functional decline and disorganization may appear during psychotic periods.
Mood disorder with psychotic features has psychosis only during mood episodes.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 467
clues: A 17-year-old student describes feeling slowed down and unable to enjoy previous routines during a student health appointment.
The person can identify a period before the episode when mood, interest, and energy were much better.
Sleep, appetite, concentration, and self-worth have changed in the same period.
Most days include depressed mood or marked loss of interest, plus guilt, fatigue, and poor concentration.
Symptoms are not limited to anxiety about one topic, traumatic reminders, or chronic personality traits.
The best-fitting diagnosis centers on a major depressive episode with distress or impairment and no manic history.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 468
clues: A 37-year-old construction worker reports withdrawal symptoms that improve after opioid use during a telehealth follow-up.
Work, family, or legal obligations are impaired.
Craving remains strong despite serious consequences.
Important activities are reduced around use.
Pain disorder alone does not explain craving and compulsive use.
The case favors Opioid Use Disorder because opioid-related impairment and loss of control are central.
cases.explanation: The case points to Opioid Use Disorder because opioid use is marked by impaired control, craving, role impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and withdrawal or tolerance features.

cases.title: Training Case 469
clues: A 63-year-old retired nurse is brought in after weeks of social withdrawal and missed obligations during an outpatient visit.
The presentation is episodic and represents a clear change from previous functioning.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
The person reports feeling worthless and has passive thoughts that life is not worth much, without psychotic symptoms.
The key clinical weight is an affective episode with neurovegetative and cognitive changes.
The final clue is a sustained depressive episode with impaired functioning and no substance or bipolar explanation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 470
clues: A 22-year-old student has dental enamel erosion, shame, and repeated attempts to stop purging during a primary care consultation.
Medical complications such as electrolyte problems or parotid swelling may appear.
Self-evaluation is strongly influenced by weight and shape.
The key cycle is binge eating followed by behaviors meant to prevent weight gain.
Purging Disorder has purging without objectively large binges.
The final clue is loss-of-control binges plus vomiting, laxatives, fasting, or excessive exercise.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bulimia Nervosa because recurrent binge eating with loss of control is followed by compensatory behaviors, with self-evaluation tied to weight or shape.

cases.title: Training Case 471
clues: A 29-year-old software analyst reports lifelong difficulty reading social cues alongside unusually focused interests during an emergency assessment.
Language level may vary, but social communication differences are persistent.
Restricted or repetitive behaviors, sensory differences, routines, or highly focused interests are present.
Conversation is one-sided or literal, and nonverbal cues are hard to interpret.
Social Anxiety Disorder begins around fear of judgment rather than early social communication differences.
The best-fitting diagnosis is early-emerging social communication differences plus restricted or repetitive patterns.
cases.explanation: The case points to Autism Spectrum Disorder because persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors are present from early development and cause functional impact.

cases.title: Training Case 472
clues: A 32-year-old assault survivor reports sleep disturbance and irritability after a traumatic event during a family-requested evaluation.
Hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, poor sleep, and anger outbursts are present.
The person avoids places, people, conversations, or sensations linked to the trauma.
Substances or medical causes do not better explain the reaction.
Acute Stress Disorder is time-limited to the first month after trauma.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a trauma-related syndrome lasting more than a month with intrusion, avoidance, mood changes, and arousal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 473
clues: A 24-year-old musician arrives speaking rapidly about a new mission that cannot wait during an emergency assessment.
Spending, sexual risk, driving, or business decisions have become reckless.
Energy is increased rather than simply mood being happy.
Irritability escalates when others challenge grand plans.
A major depressive episode is common but is not required for the diagnosis.
The best-fitting diagnosis requires at least one manic episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar I Disorder because it describes a manic episode with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, risky behavior, and marked impairment or psychosis.

cases.title: Training Case 474
clues: A 35-year-old parent describes intrusive thoughts that feel unwanted and distressing during a student health appointment.
The thoughts are ego-dystonic or resisted rather than welcomed.
The concern is not limited to appearance, hoarding, illness anxiety, or ordinary worry.
Compulsions include checking, washing, counting, ordering, reassurance-seeking, or mental reviewing.
Delusions are fixed beliefs; OCD thoughts are intrusive and repetitive even when insight is poor.
The case favors OCD rather than generalized worry because of the ritualized neutralizing behaviors.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 475
clues: A 63-year-old retiree dreads bedtime because sleep has become unpredictable during a student health appointment.
The sleep difficulty occurs repeatedly over months.
Anxiety or depression may coexist but does not fully explain the sleep complaint.
The person may sleep better away from the usual bedroom.
Circadian rhythm disorders involve misalignment of sleep timing.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty with daytime impairment despite adequate sleep opportunity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 476
clues: A 33-year-old nurse fears another sudden episode of intense physical terror during a student health appointment.
Some attacks occur unexpectedly rather than only in a specific feared situation.
Cardiac and endocrine evaluations do not explain the episodes.
The person monitors the body closely for sensations that might mean another attack is starting.
If avoidance is mainly about being unable to escape, agoraphobia may be comorbid or primary.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent unexpected panic attacks followed by worry or behavioral change.
cases.explanation: The case points to Panic Disorder because recurrent unexpected panic attacks are followed by persistent concern about attacks and avoidance of internal cues or situations.

cases.title: Training Case 477
clues: A 22-year-old graduate student presents with chronic pessimism, fatigue, and low self-confidence during a primary care consultation.
The person continues working but describes constant low energy, poor sleep, and hopelessness.
The pattern has persisted for more than two years in adulthood.
The chronic mood state is accompanied by low appetite or overeating, poor sleep, fatigue, low self-esteem, and impaired concentration.
Major depressive episodes may occur on top of the chronic pattern, but the long baseline course is the clue.
The case favors a persistent depressive pattern rather than a single isolated depressive episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 478
clues: A 35-year-old server reports chronic emptiness, abandonment panic, and impulsive crises during a primary care consultation.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
Mood shifts are rapid and reactive, often lasting hours rather than discrete mood episodes.
Fear of abandonment drives frantic efforts to prevent separation.
Bipolar II mood episodes last days and include increased energy, not only interpersonal reactivity.
The case favors Borderline Personality Disorder because the pattern is trait-like and interpersonal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 479
clues: A 45-year-old consultant describes years of alternating emotional highs and lows that never fully meet episode thresholds during a student health appointment.
There are no psychotic symptoms and no history of hospitalization for mood elevation.
The pattern has lasted for years with many periods of hypomanic symptoms and depressive symptoms.
At times the person sleeps less, talks more, and takes on projects; at other times motivation and self-esteem drop.
A full manic episode would indicate Bipolar I Disorder.
The case is tougher because it sits below the threshold for Bipolar I or II.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 480
clues: A 27-year-old resident physician reports recurrent depression and occasional weeks of feeling unusually driven during a telehealth follow-up.
Friends describe the person as more talkative, social, impulsive, and distractible during these spells.
During high-energy periods, sleep need drops and plans multiply.
The hypomanic periods are clear departures from baseline but do not become full mania.
Antidepressant activation should be distinguished from spontaneous hypomania.
The case favors Bipolar II Disorder because the elevated mood never reaches full manic severity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar II Disorder because it combines major depressive episodes with hypomanic periods that are observable and episodic but not severe enough to qualify as mania.

cases.title: Training Case 481
clues: A 33-year-old nurse fears another sudden episode of intense physical terror during a student health appointment.
Cardiac and endocrine evaluations do not explain the episodes.
Episodes include palpitations, trembling, chest tightness, dizziness, and fear of dying or losing control.
The attacks are abrupt, intense, and time-limited.
The key is recurrent unexpected attacks plus persistent concern or maladaptive behavior.
The final clue is panic surges peaking within minutes plus ongoing fear of recurrence.
cases.explanation: The case points to Panic Disorder because recurrent unexpected panic attacks are followed by persistent concern about attacks and avoidance of internal cues or situations.

cases.title: Training Case 482
clues: A 44-year-old paramedic avoids reminders of a life-threatening experience during a primary care consultation.
Hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, poor sleep, and anger outbursts are present.
The person avoids places, people, conversations, or sensations linked to the trauma.
Intrusion, avoidance, negative mood and cognition changes, and arousal symptoms occur together.
Adjustment Disorder lacks the full trauma symptom clusters.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a trauma-related syndrome lasting more than a month with intrusion, avoidance, mood changes, and arousal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 483
clues: A 28-year-old graduate student reports withdrawal symptoms that improve after opioid use during a student health appointment.
Tolerance and withdrawal may be present outside appropriate supervised treatment contexts.
Craving remains strong despite serious consequences.
Attempts to taper fail because of craving and withdrawal.
Pain disorder alone does not explain craving and compulsive use.
The final clue is opioid craving and continued use despite overdose risk and withdrawal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Opioid Use Disorder because opioid use is marked by impaired control, craving, role impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and withdrawal or tolerance features.

cases.title: Training Case 484
clues: A 40-year-old office manager has recurrent binge episodes associated with distress during a student health appointment.
Binges occur with rapid eating, eating when not hungry, eating until uncomfortably full, or eating alone from embarrassment.
Dieting attempts may alternate with binge episodes.
The binge episodes are recurrent over time.
Anorexia Nervosa involves significantly low weight and restriction.
The case favors Binge-Eating Disorder because binge episodes, not compensation, organize the presentation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 485
clues: A 36-year-old parent reports episodes of eating large amounts with loss of control during a student health appointment.
The person describes loss of control rather than ordinary overeating.
There are no regular compensatory behaviors such as vomiting, laxatives, or fasting.
The absence of compensatory behavior separates it from bulimia.
Bulimia Nervosa includes recurrent compensatory behaviors.
The case favors Binge-Eating Disorder because binge episodes, not compensation, organize the presentation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 486
clues: A 27-year-old dancer reports intense fear of weight gain despite being medically underweight during a student health appointment.
The person evaluates self-worth heavily through shape, weight, or control.
The problem is not simply low appetite from depression or fear of choking.
Calorie counting, avoidance of meals, and compensatory exercise dominate the day.
ARFID lacks fear of weight gain or body image disturbance.
The best-fitting diagnosis is restriction causing significantly low weight with fear of weight gain and body image disturbance.
cases.explanation: The case points to Anorexia Nervosa because restriction leads to significantly low weight, with fear of weight gain and disturbed body image or denial of seriousness.

cases.title: Training Case 487
clues: A 22-year-old college student reports hearing voices while self-care and work have deteriorated during a student health appointment.
Functioning at school, work, or relationships is clearly below previous levels.
Mood episodes are absent or occupy a minority of the illness.
Family reports deterioration in hygiene, social engagement, and goal-directed behavior.
Schizophreniform Disorder has a shorter course.
The best-fitting diagnosis is chronic psychosis with functional decline and negative symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophrenia because psychotic symptoms, negative symptoms, functional decline, and a prolonged course occur outside mood episodes and without a better substance or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 488
clues: A 31-year-old attorney has dental enamel erosion, shame, and repeated attempts to stop purging during an outpatient visit.
Self-evaluation is strongly influenced by weight and shape.
Medical complications such as electrolyte problems or parotid swelling may appear.
The key cycle is binge eating followed by behaviors meant to prevent weight gain.
Binge-Eating Disorder lacks recurrent compensatory behaviors.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent binge eating with compensatory behaviors and shape-weight overvaluation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bulimia Nervosa because recurrent binge eating with loss of control is followed by compensatory behaviors, with self-evaluation tied to weight or shape.

cases.title: Training Case 489
clues: A 64-year-old retiree fears having a serious illness despite minimal symptoms during a primary care consultation.
The concern is not better explained by delusional disorder or OCD.
Somatic symptoms are absent or mild.
The person overinterprets normal sensations as evidence of disease.
Generalized Anxiety Disorder includes many worry domains rather than illness preoccupation.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent fear of having or acquiring serious illness with minimal symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 490
clues: A 64-year-old retiree is preoccupied with cancer, neurologic disease, or heart disease despite reassurance during a family-requested evaluation.
The preoccupation has lasted months and reassurance fades quickly.
Medical evaluation has not supported the feared illness.
The person overinterprets normal sensations as evidence of disease.
Generalized Anxiety Disorder includes many worry domains rather than illness preoccupation.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent fear of having or acquiring serious illness with minimal symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 491
clues: A 67-year-old retiree continues drinking despite arguments, blackouts, and medical concerns during a family-requested evaluation.
Risky situations such as driving or operating equipment while intoxicated occur.
Alcohol is taken in larger amounts or for longer than intended.
The person minimizes use despite collateral concern.
Depression or anxiety may coexist but does not explain the impaired control over alcohol.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic pattern of alcohol use with impaired control, impairment, risky use, or pharmacologic features.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 492
clues: A 20-year-old student reports being seen as unpredictable, energetic for stretches, then low and withdrawn during a student health appointment.
Symptoms are present much of the time and create relationship and work problems.
The person says the shifts are more chronic than episodic major depression.
The highs and lows are subthreshold but recurrent.
The diagnosis depends on chronic subthreshold cycling.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a chronic bipolar-spectrum pattern below full mood episode thresholds.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 493
clues: A 21-year-old student fears having a serious illness despite minimal symptoms during a primary care consultation.
Medical evaluation has not supported the feared illness.
Somatic symptoms are absent or mild.
The person overinterprets normal sensations as evidence of disease.
Generalized Anxiety Disorder includes many worry domains rather than illness preoccupation.
The final clue is disease preoccupation, checking, and reassurance cycles despite little somatic burden.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 494
clues: A 34-year-old parent reports intense fear of weight gain despite being medically underweight during a student health appointment.
The problem is not simply low appetite from depression or fear of choking.
The person evaluates self-worth heavily through shape, weight, or control.
Calorie counting, avoidance of meals, and compensatory exercise dominate the day.
ARFID lacks fear of weight gain or body image disturbance.
The final clue is low weight plus persistent weight-gain fear and disturbed body experience.
cases.explanation: The case points to Anorexia Nervosa because restriction leads to significantly low weight, with fear of weight gain and disturbed body image or denial of seriousness.

cases.title: Training Case 495
clues: A 23-year-old graduate trainee reports intense fear of being judged during conversations or performance during a family-requested evaluation.
Panic symptoms can occur, but they are tied to social evaluation.
Avoidance has affected school, work, dating, or presentations.
Social or performance situations reliably trigger anxiety.
The main fear is negative evaluation rather than broad worry or panic itself.
The final clue is anxiety about scrutiny, embarrassment, and negative judgment across social situations.
cases.explanation: The case points to Social Anxiety Disorder because anxiety and avoidance center on possible negative evaluation in social or performance situations, causing impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 496
clues: A 28-year-old chef seeks help because mood instability has become a long-term pattern during a family-requested evaluation.
The person says the shifts are more chronic than episodic major depression.
Symptoms are present much of the time and create relationship and work problems.
Others notice cycles of activation and discouragement.
A full manic episode would indicate Bipolar I Disorder.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a chronic bipolar-spectrum pattern below full mood episode thresholds.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 497
clues: A 25-year-old software tester has started avoiding activities that might trigger racing heart sensations during an outpatient visit.
Episodes include palpitations, trembling, chest tightness, dizziness, and fear of dying or losing control.
Avoidance now includes exercise, caffeine, driving, or being alone.
The person monitors the body closely for sensations that might mean another attack is starting.
The key is recurrent unexpected attacks plus persistent concern or maladaptive behavior.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent unexpected panic attacks followed by worry or behavioral change.
cases.explanation: The case points to Panic Disorder because recurrent unexpected panic attacks are followed by persistent concern about attacks and avoidance of internal cues or situations.

cases.title: Training Case 498
clues: A 50-year-old train operator avoids reminders of a life-threatening experience during a primary care consultation.
Hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, poor sleep, and anger outbursts are present.
Nightmares, flashbacks, or unwanted memories recur despite efforts to avoid them.
Substances or medical causes do not better explain the reaction.
Acute Stress Disorder is time-limited to the first month after trauma.
The case favors PTSD because the symptom pattern is anchored to traumatic exposure.
cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 499
clues: A 36-year-old writer has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during an outpatient visit.
There has been a period of psychosis lasting at least two weeks without a major mood episode.
During mood episodes, psychosis intensifies, but it does not disappear completely between them.
Mood episodes are prominent across the overall illness.
Schizophrenia has mood episodes absent or brief relative to psychosis.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 500
clues: A 36-year-old parent has recurrent binge episodes associated with distress during a family-requested evaluation.
Weight can vary and is not the diagnostic requirement.
Binges occur with rapid eating, eating when not hungry, eating until uncomfortably full, or eating alone from embarrassment.
The absence of compensatory behavior separates it from bulimia.
Night Eating Syndrome has a circadian eating pattern rather than discrete loss-of-control binges.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent distressing binge eating without compensatory behavior.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 501
clues: A 36-year-old writer reports voices and paranoia along with episodes of mania or depression during an emergency assessment.
Major mood episodes are also present for a large portion of the illness.
During mood episodes, psychosis intensifies, but it does not disappear completely between them.
Functional decline and disorganization may appear during psychotic periods.
Schizophrenia has mood episodes absent or brief relative to psychosis.
The best-fitting diagnosis is psychosis outside mood episodes plus mood episodes present for much of the illness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 502
clues: A 32-year-old freelancer describes years of alternating emotional highs and lows that never fully meet episode thresholds during a primary care consultation.
The person says the shifts are more chronic than episodic major depression.
Neither the high nor low periods clearly meet full criteria for hypomanic, manic, or major depressive episodes.
The highs and lows are subthreshold but recurrent.
A full manic episode would indicate Bipolar I Disorder.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a chronic bipolar-spectrum pattern below full mood episode thresholds.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 503
clues: A 54-year-old store manager reports never feeling fully well between periods of worse depression during a telehealth follow-up.
The person continues working but describes constant low energy, poor sleep, and hopelessness.
Symptoms are less explosive than an acute episode but have become woven into daily life.
The mood problem is long-term rather than confined to a recent two-week episode.
Symptoms are not better explained by a personality disorder, substance use, or bipolar spectrum condition.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a chronic depressive disorder lasting years with ongoing impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 504
clues: A 45-year-old business owner is evaluated after repeated deceit, aggression, and legal problems during a telehealth follow-up.
There is repeated lying, impulsivity, irritability, reckless disregard, irresponsibility, or lack of remorse.
Consequences rarely change behavior.
The person minimizes harm and blames victims.
Mania produces episodic recklessness rather than a stable pattern since youth.
The case favors Antisocial Personality Disorder because the pattern is developmental and persistent.
cases.explanation: The case points to Antisocial Personality Disorder because there is a pervasive adult pattern of violating others' rights, with evidence of conduct problems before adulthood.

cases.title: Training Case 505
clues: A 25-year-old artist is evaluated for psychotic symptoms that are not confined to mood episodes during a family-requested evaluation.
The person has had syndromal mania or depression plus schizophrenia-spectrum symptoms.
Major mood episodes are also present for a large portion of the illness.
Mood episodes are prominent across the overall illness.
Bipolar I with psychotic features does not have extended psychosis outside mania or depression.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 506
clues: A 23-year-old graduate trainee avoids situations where others might notice embarrassment during an emergency assessment.
Avoidance has affected school, work, dating, or presentations.
Panic symptoms can occur, but they are tied to social evaluation.
Social or performance situations reliably trigger anxiety.
Symptoms are not simply introversion because they cause distress and impairment.
The case favors Social Anxiety Disorder because social evaluation is the organizing fear.
cases.explanation: The case points to Social Anxiety Disorder because anxiety and avoidance center on possible negative evaluation in social or performance situations, causing impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 507
clues: A 43-year-old nurse reports withdrawal symptoms that improve after opioid use during a student health appointment.
Craving remains strong despite serious consequences.
Tolerance and withdrawal may be present outside appropriate supervised treatment contexts.
Attempts to taper fail because of craving and withdrawal.
Appropriate prescribed opioid use without impaired control is not the disorder.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic opioid use pattern with impaired control, harm, craving, and possible withdrawal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Opioid Use Disorder because opioid use is marked by impaired control, craving, role impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and withdrawal or tolerance features.

cases.title: Training Case 508
clues: A 61-year-old retiree feels intense distress when family tries to discard possessions during an emergency assessment.
Fire safety, falls, or family conflict have become concerns.
The difficulty discarding has grown over years.
Discarding even low-value objects causes marked distress.
Collectors organize and display items; hoarding creates clutter and impairment.
The case favors Hoarding Disorder because clutter and saving are the central syndrome.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 509
clues: A 55-year-old teacher is referred after rooms became unusable because of saved items during a student health appointment.
Fire safety, falls, or family conflict have become concerns.
The person believes items may be needed, are sentimental, or must not be wasted.
The person resists cleanup because each item feels potentially important.
Neurocognitive disorders can cause clutter but usually involve decline and poor organization for different reasons.
The case favors Hoarding Disorder because clutter and saving are the central syndrome.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 510
clues: A 25-year-old artist is evaluated for psychotic symptoms that are not confined to mood episodes during a student health appointment.
Major mood episodes are also present for a large portion of the illness.
Substance use does not explain the psychotic course.
Functional decline and disorganization may appear during psychotic periods.
Mood disorder with psychotic features has psychosis only during mood episodes.
The best-fitting diagnosis is psychosis outside mood episodes plus mood episodes present for much of the illness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 511
clues: A 48-year-old pharmacist reports repetitive checking, washing, or mental rituals during a student health appointment.
Insight may vary, but the person is distressed by the cycle.
The rituals temporarily reduce anxiety but the relief fades quickly.
Compulsions include checking, washing, counting, ordering, reassurance-seeking, or mental reviewing.
The time-consuming ritual cycle is the diagnostic center.
The case favors OCD rather than generalized worry because of the ritualized neutralizing behaviors.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 512
clues: A 47-year-old mechanic reports voices and paranoia along with episodes of mania or depression during a family-requested evaluation.
During mood episodes, psychosis intensifies, but it does not disappear completely between them.
There has been a period of psychosis lasting at least two weeks without a major mood episode.
Mood episodes are prominent across the overall illness.
Schizophrenia has mood episodes absent or brief relative to psychosis.
The best-fitting diagnosis is psychosis outside mood episodes plus mood episodes present for much of the illness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 513
clues: A 67-year-old widower presents with chronic pessimism, fatigue, and low self-confidence during a student health appointment.
Friends say the mood problem predates the latest stressor by years.
There have not been symptom-free stretches longer than a couple of months.
The mood problem is long-term rather than confined to a recent two-week episode.
Major depressive episodes may occur on top of the chronic pattern, but the long baseline course is the clue.
The final clue is depressed mood most days across years, with low self-esteem, sleep problems, and hopelessness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 514
clues: A 64-year-old retiree fears having a serious illness despite minimal symptoms during an outpatient visit.
The preoccupation has lasted months and reassurance fades quickly.
The fear is disease-focused, not a panic surge or broad everyday worry.
Functional impairment comes from disease preoccupation.
OCD has broader intrusive thoughts and rituals.
The final clue is disease preoccupation, checking, and reassurance cycles despite little somatic burden.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 515
clues: A 35-year-old server describes intense relationships that swing between closeness and rupture during a family-requested evaluation.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
Identity, goals, and self-image feel unstable.
Fear of abandonment drives frantic efforts to prevent separation.
Bipolar II mood episodes last days and include increased energy, not only interpersonal reactivity.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a pervasive pattern of instability in relationships, self-image, affect, and impulse control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 516
clues: A 17-year-old adolescent is evaluated for long-standing social communication differences during a family-requested evaluation.
The presentation is not explained only by social fear or attention problems.
Social reciprocity, nonverbal communication, and peer relationships are affected.
Interests are intense, specific, and absorbing.
ADHD causes impulsivity and inattention but does not fully explain restricted interests and reciprocity differences.
The best-fitting diagnosis is early-emerging social communication differences plus restricted or repetitive patterns.
cases.explanation: The case points to Autism Spectrum Disorder because persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors are present from early development and cause functional impact.

cases.title: Training Case 517
clues: A 27-year-old resident physician reports recurrent depression and occasional weeks of feeling unusually driven during a telehealth follow-up.
The depressive episodes create the greater functional burden.
During high-energy periods, sleep need drops and plans multiply.
The hypomanic periods are clear departures from baseline but do not become full mania.
A single past manic episode would change the answer to Bipolar I Disorder.
The case favors Bipolar II Disorder because the elevated mood never reaches full manic severity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar II Disorder because it combines major depressive episodes with hypomanic periods that are observable and episodic but not severe enough to qualify as mania.

cases.title: Training Case 518
clues: A 28-year-old artist has become socially withdrawn and increasingly suspicious during a student health appointment.
Functioning at school, work, or relationships is clearly below previous levels.
Hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech, or disorganized behavior are prominent.
The person believes others are monitoring thoughts and hears voices commenting on actions.
Schizoaffective Disorder requires a substantial mood component plus psychosis outside mood episodes.
The best-fitting diagnosis is chronic psychosis with functional decline and negative symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophrenia because psychotic symptoms, negative symptoms, functional decline, and a prolonged course occur outside mood episodes and without a better substance or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 519
clues: A 44-year-old surgeon continues drinking despite arguments, blackouts, and medical concerns during a primary care consultation.
Alcohol is taken in larger amounts or for longer than intended.
Important activities have been reduced or abandoned.
The person minimizes use despite collateral concern.
Social drinking lacks the persistent impairment and loss of control.
The case favors Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol-related control and consequences dominate.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 520
clues: A 35-year-old technician has a persistent persecutory, jealous, grandiose, somatic, or erotomanic belief during an outpatient visit.
There is no broad disorganized speech, negative symptoms, or marked deterioration.
Hallucinations, if present, are not prominent and relate to the delusional theme.
The person acts on the belief through complaints, investigations, or confrontations.
Schizophrenia has broader psychosis and functional decline.
The case favors Delusional Disorder because the psychosis is circumscribed.
cases.explanation: The case points to Delusional Disorder because a fixed delusional belief persists while broader schizophrenia-spectrum features and marked functional decline are absent.

cases.title: Training Case 521
clues: A 24-year-old student has persistent physical symptoms with disproportionate health-related anxiety during an outpatient visit.
The person repeatedly researches symptoms and seeks consultations.
Anxiety, depression, and medical illness may coexist.
Reassurance is short-lived.
Factitious Disorder involves deception without obvious external rewards.
The final clue is disproportionate health-related preoccupation around actual symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Somatic Symptom Disorder because distressing physical symptoms are accompanied by excessive health-related thoughts, anxiety, and behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 522
clues: A 26-year-old detainee has a long pattern of violating others' rights and disregarding consequences during a telehealth follow-up.
Relationships are marked by manipulation or exploitation.
There is repeated lying, impulsivity, irritability, reckless disregard, irresponsibility, or lack of remorse.
Work, family, and legal obligations are repeatedly disregarded.
Narcissistic Personality Disorder may exploit others but does not require conduct-disorder history.
The final clue is pervasive rights violation, deceit, impulsivity, irresponsibility, and lack of remorse.
cases.explanation: The case points to Antisocial Personality Disorder because there is a pervasive adult pattern of violating others' rights, with evidence of conduct problems before adulthood.

cases.title: Training Case 523
clues: A 27-year-old resident physician describes depressive lows interrupted by briefer periods of increased confidence and energy during a family-requested evaluation.
The elevated periods last several days and are noticeable to others.
The depressive episodes create the greater functional burden.
The person may initially experience the elevated periods as useful rather than distressing.
Rapid mood shifts lasting hours point more toward personality dynamics than hypomanic episodes.
The best-fitting diagnosis combines major depression with hypomanic episodes and no history of mania.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar II Disorder because it combines major depressive episodes with hypomanic periods that are observable and episodic but not severe enough to qualify as mania.

cases.title: Training Case 524
clues: A 54-year-old store manager says low mood has felt like the default setting for years during a family-requested evaluation.
There have not been symptom-free stretches longer than a couple of months.
Friends say the mood problem predates the latest stressor by years.
The person describes being functional but persistently joyless and self-critical.
Major depressive episodes may occur on top of the chronic pattern, but the long baseline course is the clue.
The final clue is depressed mood most days across years, with low self-esteem, sleep problems, and hopelessness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 525
clues: A 44-year-old parent reports constant worry that moves from one topic to another during a primary care consultation.
The anxiety is not limited to scrutiny, contamination, trauma reminders, or a single phobic object.
Reassurance helps briefly but the worry quickly shifts to another topic.
Avoidance and reassurance-seeking are spreading across daily life.
The breadth of worry distinguishes it from phobias and social anxiety.
The case favors a generalized anxiety pattern rather than a circumscribed fear disorder.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 526
clues: A 45-year-old consultant reports being seen as unpredictable, energetic for stretches, then low and withdrawn during an outpatient visit.
Neither the high nor low periods clearly meet full criteria for hypomanic, manic, or major depressive episodes.
There are no psychotic symptoms and no history of hospitalization for mood elevation.
At times the person sleeps less, talks more, and takes on projects; at other times motivation and self-esteem drop.
A full hypomanic episode plus major depression would indicate Bipolar II Disorder.
The case is tougher because it sits below the threshold for Bipolar I or II.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 527
clues: A 39-year-old parent is evaluated after recent onset of disorganized speech and suspicious beliefs during a student health appointment.
Mood episodes do not dominate the illness.
The disturbance has lasted more than a month but less than six months.
Symptoms persist beyond a brief psychotic episode.
Schizoaffective Disorder requires a specific mood-psychosis relationship.
The case is a duration puzzle: schizophrenia-spectrum symptoms without schizophrenia duration.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophreniform Disorder because schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms have lasted longer than brief psychosis but have not reached the longer schizophrenia duration threshold.

cases.title: Training Case 528
clues: A 41-year-old accountant describes feeling slowed down and unable to enjoy previous routines during a student health appointment.
Sleep, appetite, concentration, and self-worth have changed in the same period.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
Family notes slowed speech, diminished pleasure, and difficulty making decisions.
Symptoms are not limited to anxiety about one topic, traumatic reminders, or chronic personality traits.
The best-fitting diagnosis centers on a major depressive episode with distress or impairment and no manic history.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 529
clues: A 9-year-old child has a long history of impulsivity and trouble waiting turns during a student health appointment.
Teachers, family, or coworkers describe a consistent pattern rather than a recent decline.
Hyperactivity or impulsivity includes fidgeting, interrupting, impatience, and feeling driven.
External structure helps but does not eliminate the symptoms.
Depression causes low motivation and slowed thinking during mood episodes.
The final clue is developmentally persistent attention and impulse-control symptoms with impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 530
clues: A 49-year-old attorney has daytime fatigue and irritability despite adequate chance to sleep during a family-requested evaluation.
Anxiety or depression may coexist but does not fully explain the sleep complaint.
The sleep difficulty occurs repeatedly over months.
The person may sleep better away from the usual bedroom.
Major depression has broader mood and neurovegetative symptoms.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty with daytime impairment despite adequate sleep opportunity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 531
clues: A 46-year-old parent arrives speaking rapidly about a new mission that cannot wait during an outpatient visit.
Family reports pressured speech, grandiosity, distractibility, and reduced need for sleep.
The change has lasted long enough to disrupt work, family, and safety.
The person feels unusually powerful and says sleep is unnecessary.
A major depressive episode is common but is not required for the diagnosis.
The best-fitting diagnosis requires at least one manic episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar I Disorder because it describes a manic episode with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, risky behavior, and marked impairment or psychosis.

cases.title: Training Case 532
clues: A 31-year-old attorney describes cycles of bingeing and vomiting while weight remains not significantly low during a primary care consultation.
Binge episodes involve loss of control and unusually large intake.
The person often feels guilt, secrecy, and fear of weight gain after binges.
Weight may be normal, above, or fluctuating, but not significantly low as in anorexia.
Purging Disorder has purging without objectively large binges.
The case favors Bulimia Nervosa because the binge-compensation cycle is central.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bulimia Nervosa because recurrent binge eating with loss of control is followed by compensatory behaviors, with self-evaluation tied to weight or shape.

cases.title: Training Case 533
clues: A 64-year-old retiree is preoccupied with cancer, neurologic disease, or heart disease despite reassurance during an emergency assessment.
The fear is disease-focused, not a panic surge or broad everyday worry.
The preoccupation has lasted months and reassurance fades quickly.
Functional impairment comes from disease preoccupation.
Somatic Symptom Disorder has prominent distressing symptoms.
The case favors Illness Anxiety Disorder because the feared illness is central.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 534
clues: A 22-year-old student describes intense relationships that swing between closeness and rupture during a primary care consultation.
The pattern is long-standing and appears across relationships and settings.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
Relationships alternate between idealization and devaluation.
Bipolar II mood episodes last days and include increased energy, not only interpersonal reactivity.
The final clue is abandonment fear, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, and self-harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 535
clues: A 45-year-old business owner shows little remorse after exploiting or harming others during a primary care consultation.
Consequences rarely change behavior.
The pattern began before adulthood with conduct problems.
Risk-taking continues despite danger to self or others.
Mania produces episodic recklessness rather than a stable pattern since youth.
The best-fitting diagnosis is adult antisocial behavior with evidence of conduct problems before age 15.
cases.explanation: The case points to Antisocial Personality Disorder because there is a pervasive adult pattern of violating others' rights, with evidence of conduct problems before adulthood.

cases.title: Training Case 536
clues: A 52-year-old school administrator says the mind rarely turns off even when problems are minor during a telehealth follow-up.
The person feels restless, fatigued, irritable, and unable to concentrate.
There are no recurrent unexpected panic attacks as the main problem.
The person describes excessive worry that is hard to control.
The time course distinguishes it from brief stress reactions.
The final clue is difficult-to-control worry more days than not, across multiple topics.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 537
clues: A 19-year-old trainee arrives late because rituals take hours each morning during a family-requested evaluation.
The concern is not limited to appearance, hoarding, illness anxiety, or ordinary worry.
The thoughts are ego-dystonic or resisted rather than welcomed.
Obsessions include contamination, harm, symmetry, taboo thoughts, or fear of mistakes.
The time-consuming ritual cycle is the diagnostic center.
The final clue is unwanted intrusive thoughts and repetitive rituals aimed at neutralizing anxiety.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 538
clues: A 33-year-old resident physician has a long history of impulsivity and trouble waiting turns during a telehealth follow-up.
Symptoms began in childhood and appear in more than one setting.
Hyperactivity or impulsivity includes fidgeting, interrupting, impatience, and feeling driven.
The person struggles with sustained effort even for important tasks.
Anxiety can impair concentration but is driven by worry.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a childhood-onset pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity across settings.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 539
clues: A 44-year-old parent reports constant worry that moves from one topic to another during a student health appointment.
The anxiety is not limited to scrutiny, contamination, trauma reminders, or a single phobic object.
The person feels restless, fatigued, irritable, and unable to concentrate.
The person describes excessive worry that is hard to control.
The main symptom is worry, not obsessions, panic surges, or traumatic re-experiencing.
The final clue is difficult-to-control worry more days than not, across multiple topics.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 540
clues: A 36-year-old parent reports intense yearning and daily preoccupation with a person who died during a telehealth follow-up.
The person may feel identity disruption, disbelief, bitterness, or meaninglessness tied to the death.
The person avoids reminders yet also searches for closeness to the deceased.
Daily routines are organized around the deceased.
Adjustment Disorder is less persistent and less grief-specific.
The final clue is prolonged separation distress, identity disruption, and functional impairment after bereavement.
cases.explanation: The case points to Prolonged Grief Disorder because persistent yearning and preoccupation after bereavement remain intense, impairing, and grief-specific well beyond the acute mourning period.

cases.title: Training Case 541
clues: A 29-year-old bartender has tried to cut down drinking but repeatedly returns to heavy use during an emergency assessment.
Important activities have been reduced or abandoned.
Alcohol is taken in larger amounts or for longer than intended.
Morning tremor or sweating improves after alcohol.
Depression or anxiety may coexist but does not explain the impaired control over alcohol.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic pattern of alcohol use with impaired control, impairment, risky use, or pharmacologic features.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 542
clues: A 32-year-old freelancer seeks help because mood instability has become a long-term pattern during an outpatient visit.
Neither the high nor low periods clearly meet full criteria for hypomanic, manic, or major depressive episodes.
There are no psychotic symptoms and no history of hospitalization for mood elevation.
Others notice cycles of activation and discouragement.
The diagnosis depends on chronic subthreshold cycling.
The case is tougher because it sits below the threshold for Bipolar I or II.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 543
clues: A 32-year-old assault survivor avoids reminders of a life-threatening experience during an outpatient visit.
The person avoids places, people, conversations, or sensations linked to the trauma.
Hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, poor sleep, and anger outbursts are present.
Intrusion, avoidance, negative mood and cognition changes, and arousal symptoms occur together.
Panic Disorder does not require trauma-linked intrusions and avoidance.
The final clue is trauma exposure plus re-experiencing, avoidance, negative mood, hyperarousal, and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 544
clues: A 55-year-old technician reports binges of stimulant use followed by crashes and failed attempts to stop during an outpatient visit.
Sleep and appetite are disrupted.
Tolerance, risky use, and continued use despite harm may be present.
Attempts to quit trigger dysphoria, fatigue, and craving.
Primary psychotic disorder persists beyond substance effects.
The final clue is stimulant binges with insomnia, crash, paranoia, and loss of control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 545
clues: A 44-year-old surgeon misses obligations and hides bottles despite consequences during a telehealth follow-up.
Craving, time spent drinking or recovering, and role impairment are present.
Risky situations such as driving or operating equipment while intoxicated occur.
The person minimizes use despite collateral concern.
Sedative use disorder involves a different substance class.
The final clue is continued alcohol use despite harm plus tolerance or withdrawal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 546
clues: A 21-year-old student fears having a serious illness despite minimal symptoms during a family-requested evaluation.
The preoccupation has lasted months and reassurance fades quickly.
The concern is not better explained by delusional disorder or OCD.
Health-related checking, reassurance-seeking, or avoidance is prominent.
OCD has broader intrusive thoughts and rituals.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent fear of having or acquiring serious illness with minimal symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 547
clues: A 22-year-old graduate student presents with chronic pessimism, fatigue, and low self-confidence during a student health appointment.
There is no history of mania or hypomania.
The pattern has persisted for more than two years in adulthood.
The mood problem is long-term rather than confined to a recent two-week episode.
The core issue is duration and persistence of depressed mood.
The final clue is depressed mood most days across years, with low self-esteem, sleep problems, and hopelessness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 548
clues: A 30-year-old attorney reports intense fear of being judged during conversations or performance during an outpatient visit.
Avoidance has affected school, work, dating, or presentations.
The person recognizes that the feared judgment is excessive but feels unable to control the anxiety.
Social or performance situations reliably trigger anxiety.
The main fear is negative evaluation rather than broad worry or panic itself.
The final clue is anxiety about scrutiny, embarrassment, and negative judgment across social situations.
cases.explanation: The case points to Social Anxiety Disorder because anxiety and avoidance center on possible negative evaluation in social or performance situations, causing impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 549
clues: A 54-year-old store manager reports never feeling fully well between periods of worse depression during an outpatient visit.
Symptoms are less explosive than an acute episode but have become woven into daily life.
Friends say the mood problem predates the latest stressor by years.
The mood problem is long-term rather than confined to a recent two-week episode.
Symptoms are not better explained by a personality disorder, substance use, or bipolar spectrum condition.
The final clue is depressed mood most days across years, with low self-esteem, sleep problems, and hopelessness.

cases.title: Training Case 550
clues: A 55-year-old chef has recurrent binge episodes associated with distress during a family-requested evaluation.
There are no regular compensatory behaviors such as vomiting, laxatives, or fasting.
Dieting attempts may alternate with binge episodes.
The binge episodes are recurrent over time.
Bulimia Nervosa includes recurrent compensatory behaviors.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent distressing binge eating without compensatory behavior.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 551
clues: A 52-year-old executive is preoccupied with cancer, neurologic disease, or heart disease despite reassurance during a telehealth follow-up.

The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent fear of having or acquiring serious illness with minimal symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 552
clues: A 27-year-old veteran has intrusive memories that make the event feel present again during an emergency assessment.

cases.title: Training Case 553
clues: A 19-year-old college student is seen after marked weight loss and rigid food rules during a telehealth follow-up.

The best-fitting diagnosis is restriction causing significantly low weight with fear of weight gain and body image disturbance.

cases.title: Training Case 554
clues: A 43-year-old mechanic keeps newspapers, containers, clothing, and broken objects for possible future use during a telehealth follow-up.

The person believes items may be needed, are sentimental, or must not be wasted.

cases.title: Training Case 557

cases.title: Training Case 558

cases.title: Training Case 564

cases.title: Training Case 565
clues: A 20-year-old college student has restricted eating because of sensory aversion, low interest, or fear of consequences during an outpatient visit.

The best-fitting diagnosis is a chronic depressive disorder lasting years with ongoing impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

The time-consuming ritual cycle is the diagnostic center.
The best-fitting diagnosis is obsessions plus compulsions that are distressing, time-consuming, or impairing.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 588
clues: A 29-year-old collector keeps newspapers, containers, clothing, and broken objects for possible future use during a telehealth follow-up.

The person believes items may be needed, are sentimental, or must not be wasted.

cases.title: Training Case 598
