cases.explanation: The case points to Prolonged Grief Disorder because persistent yearning and preoccupation after bereavement remain intense, impairing, and grief-specific well beyond the acute mourning period.

cases.title: Training Case 599
clues: A 52-year-old sales manager has tried to cut down drinking but repeatedly returns to heavy use during a family-requested evaluation.
Important activities have been reduced or abandoned.
Craving, time spent drinking or recovering, and role impairment are present.
Morning tremor or sweating improves after alcohol.
Social drinking lacks the persistent impairment and loss of control.
The case favors Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol-related control and consequences dominate.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 600
clues: A 17-year-old adolescent is evaluated for long-standing social communication differences during a primary care consultation.
The person may mask symptoms but becomes exhausted by social demands.
Features were present in early development, even if recognized later.
Changes in routine trigger disproportionate distress.
Social Anxiety Disorder begins around fear of judgment rather than early social communication differences.
The case favors Autism Spectrum Disorder because both social communication and restricted behavior domains are present.
cases.explanation: The case points to Autism Spectrum Disorder because persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors are present from early development and cause functional impact.

cases.title: Training Case 601
clues: A 61-year-old retiree feels intense distress when family tries to discard possessions during a primary care consultation.
Acquiring items may be excessive but the key problem is discarding.
Living areas are cluttered enough that normal activities are blocked.
The person resists cleanup because each item feels potentially important.
Neurocognitive disorders can cause clutter but usually involve decline and poor organization for different reasons.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent difficulty discarding possessions with clutter and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 602
clues: A 25-year-old artist has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during an outpatient visit.
Substance use does not explain the psychotic course.
The longitudinal pattern is more than mood disorder with psychotic features.
Functional decline and disorganization may appear during psychotic periods.
Mood disorder with psychotic features has psychosis only during mood episodes.
The best-fitting diagnosis is psychosis outside mood episodes plus mood episodes present for much of the illness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 603
clues: A 23-year-old graduate trainee has turned down opportunities because of scrutiny fears during a telehealth follow-up.
Avoidance has affected school, work, dating, or presentations.
The person recognizes that the feared judgment is excessive but feels unable to control the anxiety.
Social or performance situations reliably trigger anxiety.
Symptoms are not simply introversion because they cause distress and impairment.
The final clue is anxiety about scrutiny, embarrassment, and negative judgment across social situations.
cases.explanation: The case points to Social Anxiety Disorder because anxiety and avoidance center on possible negative evaluation in social or performance situations, causing impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 604
clues: A 30-year-old delivery driver continues stimulant use despite chest pain, job loss, and relationship conflict during a family-requested evaluation.
Use occurs in larger amounts or longer runs than intended.
The person may have stimulant-induced paranoia, tactile sensations, or mood crashes.
Attempts to quit trigger dysphoria, fatigue, and craving.
ADHD treatment taken as prescribed is not stimulant use disorder.
The final clue is stimulant binges with insomnia, crash, paranoia, and loss of control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 605
clues: A 19-year-old student avoids mirrors and photos because of distress about appearance during a student health appointment.
The concern is not primarily about body weight or fatness.
The concern is repetitive, intrusive, and hard to resist.
The person repeatedly checks mirrors, searches online procedures, or compares features with others.
Delusional intensity can occur, but the diagnosis remains BDD when the theme is appearance.
The best-fitting diagnosis is an appearance preoccupation with repetitive behaviors and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 606
clues: A 24-year-old student reports pain or fatigue that has taken over daily life during a family-requested evaluation.
The symptoms are not intentionally produced.
The person repeatedly researches symptoms and seeks consultations.
Functional impairment comes from health-related thoughts, feelings, and behaviors.
Panic Disorder involves abrupt attacks rather than persistent symptom preoccupation.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent somatic symptoms plus excessive thoughts, anxiety, or behaviors about them.
cases.explanation: The case points to Somatic Symptom Disorder because distressing physical symptoms are accompanied by excessive health-related thoughts, anxiety, and behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 607
clues: A 23-year-old student has recurrent binge episodes associated with distress during a family-requested evaluation.
Dieting attempts may alternate with binge episodes.
There are no regular compensatory behaviors such as vomiting, laxatives, or fasting.
The binge episodes are recurrent over time.
Anorexia Nervosa involves significantly low weight and restriction.
The case favors Binge-Eating Disorder because binge episodes, not compensation, organize the presentation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 608
clues: A 28-year-old artist presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during a telehealth follow-up.
Identity, goals, and self-image feel unstable.
Recurrent self-harm, suicidal gestures, or threats occur during crises.
Anger, emptiness, and identity disturbance are prominent.
PTSD is trauma-anchored with intrusion and avoidance clusters.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a pervasive pattern of instability in relationships, self-image, affect, and impulse control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 609
clues: A 18-year-old student presents with muscle tension, poor sleep, and difficulty controlling worry during a student health appointment.
Medical causes and stimulant use have been considered.
The worry has been present for months and spans health, money, work, family, and small decisions.
The person describes excessive worry that is hard to control.
The time course distinguishes it from brief stress reactions.
The final clue is difficult-to-control worry more days than not, across multiple topics.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 610
clues: A 47-year-old mechanic is evaluated for psychotic symptoms that are not confined to mood episodes during a family-requested evaluation.
There has been a period of psychosis lasting at least two weeks without a major mood episode.
The person has had syndromal mania or depression plus schizophrenia-spectrum symptoms.
Delusions or hallucinations persist outside mood episodes.
Schizophrenia has mood episodes absent or brief relative to psychosis.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 611
clues: A 35-year-old parent arrives late because rituals take hours each morning during a family-requested evaluation.
The pattern consumes significant time and interferes with school, work, or relationships.
The concern is not limited to appearance, hoarding, illness anxiety, or ordinary worry.
Obsessions include contamination, harm, symmetry, taboo thoughts, or fear of mistakes.
The time-consuming ritual cycle is the diagnostic center.
The final clue is unwanted intrusive thoughts and repetitive rituals aimed at neutralizing anxiety.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 612
clues: A 29-year-old teacher is brought in after weeks of social withdrawal and missed obligations during an emergency assessment.
Sleep, appetite, concentration, and self-worth have changed in the same period.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
Family notes slowed speech, diminished pleasure, and difficulty making decisions.
Symptoms are not limited to anxiety about one topic, traumatic reminders, or chronic personality traits.
The final clue is a sustained depressive episode with impaired functioning and no substance or bipolar explanation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 613
clues: A 24-year-old student uses cocaine or methamphetamine despite paranoia, insomnia, and missed obligations during an emergency assessment.
Sleep and appetite are disrupted.
Use occurs in larger amounts or longer runs than intended.
Attempts to quit trigger dysphoria, fatigue, and craving.
ADHD treatment taken as prescribed is not stimulant use disorder.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic stimulant use pattern with craving, impairment, risky use, and continued use despite harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 614
clues: A 54-year-old store manager says low mood has felt like the default setting for years during a telehealth follow-up.
There is no history of mania or hypomania.
The pattern has persisted for more than two years in adulthood.
The chronic mood state is accompanied by low appetite or overeating, poor sleep, fatigue, low self-esteem, and impaired concentration.
The core issue is duration and persistence of depressed mood.
The case favors a persistent depressive pattern rather than a single isolated depressive episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 615
clues: A 21-year-old student fears having a serious illness despite minimal symptoms during a primary care consultation.
Somatic symptoms are absent or mild.
The concern is not better explained by delusional disorder or OCD.
The person overinterprets normal sensations as evidence of disease.
Generalized Anxiety Disorder includes many worry domains rather than illness preoccupation.
The final clue is disease preoccupation, checking, and reassurance cycles despite little somatic burden.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 616
clues: A 47-year-old mechanic has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during a family-requested evaluation.
During mood episodes, psychosis intensifies, but it does not disappear completely between them.
There has been a period of psychosis lasting at least two weeks without a major mood episode.
Delusions or hallucinations persist outside mood episodes.
Mood disorder with psychotic features has psychosis only during mood episodes.
The case favors Schizoaffective Disorder because neither schizophrenia nor mood disorder alone accounts for the timeline.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 617
clues: A 28-year-old nurse has daytime fatigue and irritability despite adequate chance to sleep during a family-requested evaluation.
The sleep difficulty occurs repeatedly over months.
Caffeine, shift work, substances, and untreated sleep apnea are assessed.
Poor sleep becomes a conditioned cycle.
Circadian rhythm disorders involve misalignment of sleep timing.
The final clue is chronic insomnia symptoms with functional consequences and no better primary sleep-wake explanation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 618
clues: A 58-year-old widow reports intense yearning and daily preoccupation with a person who died during an outpatient visit.
Work, relationships, and future planning remain impaired.
The person may feel identity disruption, disbelief, bitterness, or meaninglessness tied to the death.
The person denies a wish to die but says life feels empty without the deceased.
Major depression is broader and not necessarily centered on separation distress.
The final clue is prolonged separation distress, identity disruption, and functional impairment after bereavement.
cases.explanation: The case points to Prolonged Grief Disorder because persistent yearning and preoccupation after bereavement remain intense, impairing, and grief-specific well beyond the acute mourning period.

cases.title: Training Case 619
clues: A 35-year-old server describes intense relationships that swing between closeness and rupture during a student health appointment.
The pattern is long-standing and appears across relationships and settings.
Recurrent self-harm, suicidal gestures, or threats occur during crises.
Fear of abandonment drives frantic efforts to prevent separation.
Bipolar II mood episodes last days and include increased energy, not only interpersonal reactivity.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a pervasive pattern of instability in relationships, self-image, affect, and impulse control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 620
clues: A 22-year-old graduate student reports never feeling fully well between periods of worse depression during a family-requested evaluation.
There is no history of mania or hypomania.
There have not been symptom-free stretches longer than a couple of months.
The person describes being functional but persistently joyless and self-critical.
Major depressive episodes may occur on top of the chronic pattern, but the long baseline course is the clue.
The case favors a persistent depressive pattern rather than a single isolated depressive episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 621
clues: A 51-year-old trainer seeks repeated reassurance and cosmetic opinions about one body area during a primary care consultation.
Checking, grooming, comparing, camouflaging, or reassurance-seeking takes substantial time.
The concern is not primarily about body weight or fatness.
Social avoidance comes from fear that others will notice the perceived defect.
OCD can include appearance obsessions, but BDD is specifically about perceived defects.
The final clue is distress over a perceived physical flaw with checking, comparing, and camouflaging.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 622
clues: A 45-year-old business owner is evaluated after repeated deceit, aggression, and legal problems during a student health appointment.
The person is at least an adult and the behavior is not limited to mania or psychosis.
Relationships are marked by manipulation or exploitation.
Work, family, and legal obligations are repeatedly disregarded.
Narcissistic Personality Disorder may exploit others but does not require conduct-disorder history.
The case favors Antisocial Personality Disorder because the pattern is developmental and persistent.
cases.explanation: The case points to Antisocial Personality Disorder because there is a pervasive adult pattern of violating others' rights, with evidence of conduct problems before adulthood.

cases.title: Training Case 623
clues: A 63-year-old retired nurse describes feeling slowed down and unable to enjoy previous routines during a primary care consultation.
The person can identify a period before the episode when mood, interest, and energy were much better.
The presentation is episodic and represents a clear change from previous functioning.
Most days include depressed mood or marked loss of interest, plus guilt, fatigue, and poor concentration.
The episode is more time-limited and symptom-dense than a lifelong low mood pattern.
The final clue is a sustained depressive episode with impaired functioning and no substance or bipolar explanation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 624
clues: A 24-year-old musician has become unusually energetic, irritable, and hard to interrupt during a student health appointment.
Energy is increased rather than simply mood being happy.
Family reports pressured speech, grandiosity, distractibility, and reduced need for sleep.
Ideas shift quickly, speech is pressured, and judgment is markedly impaired.
A major depressive episode is common but is not required for the diagnosis.
The episode crosses the threshold from hypomania into mania.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar I Disorder because it describes a manic episode with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, risky behavior, and marked impairment or psychosis.

cases.title: Training Case 625
clues: A 47-year-old mechanic has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during a primary care consultation.
During mood episodes, psychosis intensifies, but it does not disappear completely between them.
Major mood episodes are also present for a large portion of the illness.
Mood episodes are prominent across the overall illness.
Bipolar I with psychotic features does not have extended psychosis outside mania or depression.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 626
clues: A 36-year-old parent has been unable to re-engage with life long after a bereavement during a family-requested evaluation.
The loss occurred well beyond the expected acute mourning period.
The person may feel identity disruption, disbelief, bitterness, or meaninglessness tied to the death.
The person denies a wish to die but says life feels empty without the deceased.
Adjustment Disorder is less persistent and less grief-specific.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent, impairing grief with yearning and preoccupation long after the death.
cases.explanation: The case points to Prolonged Grief Disorder because persistent yearning and preoccupation after bereavement remain intense, impairing, and grief-specific well beyond the acute mourning period.

cases.title: Training Case 627
clues: A 41-year-old parent reports chronic emptiness, abandonment panic, and impulsive crises during a family-requested evaluation.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
Identity, goals, and self-image feel unstable.
Anger, emptiness, and identity disturbance are prominent.
PTSD is trauma-anchored with intrusion and avoidance clusters.
The case favors Borderline Personality Disorder because the pattern is trait-like and interpersonal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 628
clues: A 21-year-old athlete describes depressive lows interrupted by briefer periods of increased confidence and energy during a telehealth follow-up.
The person remembers starting multiple projects and spending more than usual.
The elevated periods last several days and are noticeable to others.
There is a history of major depressive episodes.
Rapid mood shifts lasting hours point more toward personality dynamics than hypomanic episodes.
The best-fitting diagnosis combines major depression with hypomanic episodes and no history of mania.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar II Disorder because it combines major depressive episodes with hypomanic periods that are observable and episodic but not severe enough to qualify as mania.

cases.title: Training Case 629
clues: A 57-year-old driver has started avoiding activities that might trigger racing heart sensations during a family-requested evaluation.
The fear is of the panic sensations and consequences, not one object or social judgment.
Some attacks occur unexpectedly rather than only in a specific feared situation.
Anticipatory anxiety about future attacks has become disabling.
The key is recurrent unexpected attacks plus persistent concern or maladaptive behavior.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent unexpected panic attacks followed by worry or behavioral change.
cases.explanation: The case points to Panic Disorder because recurrent unexpected panic attacks are followed by persistent concern about attacks and avoidance of internal cues or situations.

cases.title: Training Case 630
clues: A 67-year-old retiree misses obligations and hides bottles despite consequences during a telehealth follow-up.
Craving, time spent drinking or recovering, and role impairment are present.
Use continues despite interpersonal, occupational, or physical harm.
Attempts to stop are short-lived.
Sedative use disorder involves a different substance class.
The final clue is continued alcohol use despite harm plus tolerance or withdrawal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 631
clues: A 10-year-old child has difficulty with reciprocal conversation and intense distress with routine changes during a telehealth follow-up.
Restricted or repetitive behaviors, sensory differences, routines, or highly focused interests are present.
Language level may vary, but social communication differences are persistent.
Conversation is one-sided or literal, and nonverbal cues are hard to interpret.
ADHD causes impulsivity and inattention but does not fully explain restricted interests and reciprocity differences.
The case favors Autism Spectrum Disorder because both social communication and restricted behavior domains are present.
cases.explanation: The case points to Autism Spectrum Disorder because persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors are present from early development and cause functional impact.

cases.title: Training Case 632
clues: A 32-year-old assault survivor has intrusive memories that make the event feel present again during a telehealth follow-up.
Negative mood, guilt, detachment, or distorted blame has developed after the trauma.
Nightmares, flashbacks, or unwanted memories recur despite efforts to avoid them.
Substances or medical causes do not better explain the reaction.
Panic Disorder does not require trauma-linked intrusions and avoidance.
The final clue is trauma exposure plus re-experiencing, avoidance, negative mood, hyperarousal, and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 633
clues: A 22-year-old student presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during a telehealth follow-up.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
Identity, goals, and self-image feel unstable.
Anger, emptiness, and identity disturbance are prominent.
PTSD is trauma-anchored with intrusion and avoidance clusters.
The final clue is abandonment fear, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, and self-harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 634
clues: A 24-year-old model avoids mirrors and photos because of distress about appearance during an emergency assessment.
Medical examination does not match the intensity of the concern.
The concern is repetitive, intrusive, and hard to resist.
Social avoidance comes from fear that others will notice the perceived defect.
Eating disorders center on weight or shape concerns with eating behavior disturbance.
The best-fitting diagnosis is an appearance preoccupation with repetitive behaviors and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 635
clues: A 52-year-old sales manager has tried to cut down drinking but repeatedly returns to heavy use during a student health appointment.
Tolerance or withdrawal symptoms may appear.
Risky situations such as driving or operating equipment while intoxicated occur.
Morning tremor or sweating improves after alcohol.
Depression or anxiety may coexist but does not explain the impaired control over alcohol.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic pattern of alcohol use with impaired control, impairment, risky use, or pharmacologic features.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 636
clues: A 63-year-old retired nurse is brought in after weeks of social withdrawal and missed obligations during a student health appointment.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
Symptoms have clustered for more than two weeks rather than appearing as brief mood shifts.
Most days include depressed mood or marked loss of interest, plus guilt, fatigue, and poor concentration.
Symptoms are not limited to anxiety about one topic, traumatic reminders, or chronic personality traits.
The final clue is a sustained depressive episode with impaired functioning and no substance or bipolar explanation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 637
clues: A 34-year-old designer reports recurrent depression and occasional weeks of feeling unusually driven during an emergency assessment.
The elevated periods last several days and are noticeable to others.
Friends describe the person as more talkative, social, impulsive, and distractible during these spells.
There is a history of major depressive episodes.
Antidepressant activation should be distinguished from spontaneous hypomania.
The best-fitting diagnosis combines major depression with hypomanic episodes and no history of mania.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar II Disorder because it combines major depressive episodes with hypomanic periods that are observable and episodic but not severe enough to qualify as mania.

cases.title: Training Case 638
clues: A 26-year-old medical student arrives late because rituals take hours each morning during a student health appointment.
The rituals temporarily reduce anxiety but the relief fades quickly.
Insight may vary, but the person is distressed by the cycle.
Avoidance grows around triggers that set off obsessions.
The time-consuming ritual cycle is the diagnostic center.
The best-fitting diagnosis is obsessions plus compulsions that are distressing, time-consuming, or impairing.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 639
clues: A 67-year-old retiree has tried to cut down drinking but repeatedly returns to heavy use during an outpatient visit.
Important activities have been reduced or abandoned.
Craving, time spent drinking or recovering, and role impairment are present.
The person minimizes use despite collateral concern.
Sedative use disorder involves a different substance class.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic pattern of alcohol use with impaired control, impairment, risky use, or pharmacologic features.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 640
clues: A 29-year-old graduate student has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during a primary care consultation.
During mood episodes, psychosis intensifies, but it does not disappear completely between them.
Major mood episodes are also present for a large portion of the illness.
Functional decline and disorganization may appear during psychotic periods.
Mood disorder with psychotic features has psychosis only during mood episodes.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 641
clues: A 22-year-old graduate student reports never feeling fully well between periods of worse depression during an emergency assessment.
Friends say the mood problem predates the latest stressor by years.
There have not been symptom-free stretches longer than a couple of months.
The chronic mood state is accompanied by low appetite or overeating, poor sleep, fatigue, low self-esteem, and impaired concentration.
Symptoms are not better explained by a personality disorder, substance use, or bipolar spectrum condition.
The case favors a persistent depressive pattern rather than a single isolated depressive episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 642
clues: A 21-year-old student is preoccupied with cancer, neurologic disease, or heart disease despite reassurance during a family-requested evaluation.
The preoccupation has lasted months and reassurance fades quickly.
Medical evaluation has not supported the feared illness.
The person overinterprets normal sensations as evidence of disease.

cases.title: Training Case 643
clues: A 41-year-old accountant describes feeling slowed down and unable to enjoy previous routines during an emergency assessment.
The presentation is episodic and represents a clear change from previous functioning.
Alcohol, stimulants, bereavement alone, and thyroid disease do not better explain the syndrome.
Family notes slowed speech, diminished pleasure, and difficulty making decisions.
The key clinical weight is an affective episode with neurovegetative and cognitive changes.
The syndrome is best understood as a unipolar depressive episode rather than a primary anxiety, trauma, or psychotic disorder.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 644
clues: A 60-year-old mechanic spends hours monitoring bodily sensations and seeking reassurance during a student health appointment.
Time, energy, and emotion devoted to symptoms are high.
Anxiety, depression, and medical illness may coexist.
Functional impairment comes from health-related thoughts, feelings, and behaviors.

The final clue is disproportionate health-related preoccupation around actual symptoms.

cases.title: Training Case 645
clues: A 32-year-old freelancer describes years of alternating emotional highs and lows that never fully meet episode thresholds during an outpatient visit.
Neither the high nor low periods clearly meet full criteria for hypomanic, manic, or major depressive episodes.
There are no psychotic symptoms and no history of hospitalization for mood elevation.
The highs and lows are subthreshold but recurrent.
The diagnosis depends on chronic subthreshold cycling.

cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 646
clues: A 28-year-old graduate student continues opioid use despite overdoses, cravings, and failed attempts to stop during a student health appointment.
Use has escalated and control has diminished.
Pain may have started opioid exposure, but addiction features now dominate.
Important activities are reduced around use.
Pain disorder alone does not explain craving and compulsive use.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic opioid use pattern with impaired control, harm, craving, and possible withdrawal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Opioid Use Disorder because opioid use is marked by impaired control, craving, role impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and withdrawal or tolerance features.

cases.title: Training Case 647
clues: A 15-year-old student reports unfinished tasks, missed deadlines, and difficulty sustaining attention during a student health appointment.
The symptoms cause school, work, relationship, or safety impairment.
Inattention includes losing items, careless mistakes, distractibility, and difficulty finishing tasks.
The person struggles with sustained effort even for important tasks.
Autism includes social communication and restricted behavior patterns beyond attention symptoms.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a childhood-onset pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity across settings.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 648
clues: A 24-year-old student spends hours monitoring bodily sensations and seeking reassurance during an emergency assessment.
Medical findings may be present or absent, but the response is excessive and impairing.
Anxiety, depression, and medical illness may coexist.
Functional impairment comes from health-related thoughts, feelings, and behaviors.
Illness Anxiety Disorder has minimal or no somatic symptoms.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent somatic symptoms plus excessive thoughts, anxiety, or behaviors about them.
cases.explanation: The case points to Somatic Symptom Disorder because distressing physical symptoms are accompanied by excessive health-related thoughts, anxiety, and behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 649
clues: A 47-year-old mechanic has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during a telehealth follow-up.
During mood episodes, psychosis intensifies, but it does not disappear completely between them.
The longitudinal pattern is more than mood disorder with psychotic features.
Mood episodes are prominent across the overall illness.
Schizophrenia has mood episodes absent or brief relative to psychosis.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 650
clues: A 52-year-old school administrator says the mind rarely turns off even when problems are minor during a primary care consultation.
The anxiety is not limited to scrutiny, contamination, trauma reminders, or a single phobic object.
The worry has been present for months and spans health, money, work, family, and small decisions.
Avoidance and reassurance-seeking are spreading across daily life.
The breadth of worry distinguishes it from phobias and social anxiety.
The best-fitting diagnosis is chronic excessive worry across many domains with physical tension and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 651
clues: A 63-year-old retiree reports persistent difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking too early during an emergency assessment.
Daytime consequences include fatigue, poor concentration, mood irritability, or work impairment.
The person spends increasing time in bed trying to force sleep.
Poor sleep becomes a conditioned cycle.
Circadian rhythm disorders involve misalignment of sleep timing.
The final clue is chronic insomnia symptoms with functional consequences and no better primary sleep-wake explanation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 652
clues: A 30-year-old attorney has turned down opportunities because of scrutiny fears during a student health appointment.
The fear has persisted for months and is out of proportion to the actual social threat.
The person recognizes that the feared judgment is excessive but feels unable to control the anxiety.
Social or performance situations reliably trigger anxiety.
Symptoms are not simply introversion because they cause distress and impairment.
The case favors Social Anxiety Disorder because social evaluation is the organizing fear.
cases.explanation: The case points to Social Anxiety Disorder because anxiety and avoidance center on possible negative evaluation in social or performance situations, causing impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 653
clues: A 22-year-old graduate student says low mood has felt like the default setting for years during a family-requested evaluation.
There is no history of mania or hypomania.
There have not been symptom-free stretches longer than a couple of months.
The mood problem is long-term rather than confined to a recent two-week episode.
Major depressive episodes may occur on top of the chronic pattern, but the long baseline course is the clue.
The case favors a persistent depressive pattern rather than a single isolated depressive episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 654
clues: A 31-year-old nurse reports pain or fatigue that has taken over daily life during an emergency assessment.
The focus is on distressing symptoms themselves rather than fear of having a hidden disease with few symptoms.
Time, energy, and emotion devoted to symptoms are high.
The person interprets ordinary sensations as dangerous and reorganizes life around symptoms.

The final clue is disproportionate health-related preoccupation around actual symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Somatic Symptom Disorder because distressing physical symptoms are accompanied by excessive health-related thoughts, anxiety, and behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 655
clues: A 29-year-old collector is referred after rooms became unusable because of saved items during an emergency assessment.
The person believes items may be needed, are sentimental, or must not be wasted.
Fire safety, falls, or family conflict have become concerns.
The person resists cleanup because each item feels potentially important.
Collectors organize and display items; hoarding creates clutter and impairment.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent difficulty discarding possessions with clutter and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 656
clues: A 42-year-old project manager has a long history of impulsivity and trouble waiting turns during a telehealth follow-up.
Symptoms began in childhood and appear in more than one setting.
Inattention includes losing items, careless mistakes, distractibility, and difficulty finishing tasks.
Problems are developmental rather than new-onset in adulthood.
Anxiety can impair concentration but is driven by worry.
The final clue is developmentally persistent attention and impulse-control symptoms with impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 657
clues: A 45-year-old consultant seeks help because mood instability has become a long-term pattern during an outpatient visit.

There are no psychotic symptoms and no history of hospitalization for mood elevation.

cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 658
clues: A 35-year-old technician seeks help because one fixed belief has dominated life for months during an outpatient visit.
The belief has persisted for at least a month.
Mood episodes are absent or brief relative to the delusional period.
The person acts on the belief through complaints, investigations, or confrontations.
Schizophrenia has broader psychosis and functional decline.
The final clue is fixed delusional belief without schizophrenia-like disorganization or negative symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Delusional Disorder because a fixed delusional belief persists while broader schizophrenia-spectrum features and marked functional decline are absent.

cases.title: Training Case 659
clues: A 47-year-old sibling reports intense yearning and daily preoccupation with a person who died during a telehealth follow-up.
The dominant emotion is yearning, longing, or preoccupation rather than generalized low mood alone.
The person avoids reminders yet also searches for closeness to the deceased.
The person denies a wish to die but says life feels empty without the deceased.
Adjustment Disorder is less persistent and less grief-specific.
The case favors Prolonged Grief Disorder because grief-specific longing remains the central syndrome.
cases.explanation: The case points to Prolonged Grief Disorder because persistent yearning and preoccupation after bereavement remain intense, impairing, and grief-specific well beyond the acute mourning period.

cases.title: Training Case 660
clues: A 35-year-old technician holds a fixed belief that is not shared by others but otherwise appears organized during a telehealth follow-up.
Mood episodes are absent or brief relative to the delusional period.
Functioning outside the delusional topic is relatively preserved.
The person acts on the belief through complaints, investigations, or confrontations.
Schizophrenia has broader psychosis and functional decline.
The case favors Delusional Disorder because the psychosis is circumscribed.
cases.explanation: The case points to Delusional Disorder because a fixed delusional belief persists while broader schizophrenia-spectrum features and marked functional decline are absent.

cases.title: Training Case 661
clues: A 28-year-old graduate student uses more pills or heroin than intended and spends the day obtaining or recovering during a primary care consultation.
Tolerance and withdrawal may be present outside appropriate supervised treatment contexts.
Craving remains strong despite serious consequences.
Attempts to taper fail because of craving and withdrawal.
Appropriate prescribed opioid use without impaired control is not the disorder.
The final clue is opioid craving and continued use despite overdose risk and withdrawal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Opioid Use Disorder because opioid use is marked by impaired control, craving, role impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and withdrawal or tolerance features.

cases.title: Training Case 662
clues: A 34-year-old warehouse worker has become socially withdrawn and increasingly suspicious during an emergency assessment.
Hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech, or disorganized behavior are prominent.
Negative symptoms such as reduced speech, low motivation, or flat affect are present.
The person believes others are monitoring thoughts and hears voices commenting on actions.
Schizophreniform Disorder has a shorter course.
The final clue is psychosis lasting months with deterioration and symptoms not restricted to mood episodes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophrenia because psychotic symptoms, negative symptoms, functional decline, and a prolonged course occur outside mood episodes and without a better substance or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 663
clues: A 27-year-old resident physician describes depressive lows interrupted by briefer periods of increased confidence and energy during a primary care consultation.
The person remembers starting multiple projects and spending more than usual.
The elevated periods last several days and are noticeable to others.
There is a history of major depressive episodes.
Antidepressant activation should be distinguished from spontaneous hypomania.
The case favors Bipolar II Disorder because the elevated mood never reaches full manic severity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar II Disorder because it combines major depressive episodes with hypomanic periods that are observable and episodic but not severe enough to qualify as mania.

cases.title: Training Case 664
clues: A 34-year-old designer reports recurrent depression and occasional weeks of feeling unusually driven during a student health appointment.
Friends describe the person as more talkative, social, impulsive, and distractible during these spells.
The elevated periods do not cause hospitalization or psychosis.
The person may initially experience the elevated periods as useful rather than distressing.
Rapid mood shifts lasting hours point more toward personality dynamics than hypomanic episodes.
The final clue is hypomania plus major depressive episodes, without psychosis or hospitalization during elevation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar II Disorder because it combines major depressive episodes with hypomanic periods that are observable and episodic but not severe enough to qualify as mania.

cases.title: Training Case 665
clues: A 18-year-old gymnast describes cycles of bingeing and vomiting while weight remains not significantly low during a telehealth follow-up.
Medical complications such as electrolyte problems or parotid swelling may appear.
Self-evaluation is strongly influenced by weight and shape.
The person feels unable to control the binge-purge cycle.
Binge-Eating Disorder lacks recurrent compensatory behaviors.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent binge eating with compensatory behaviors and shape-weight overvaluation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bulimia Nervosa because recurrent binge eating with loss of control is followed by compensatory behaviors, with self-evaluation tied to weight or shape.

cases.title: Training Case 666
clues: A 49-year-old attorney dreads bedtime because sleep has become unpredictable during a telehealth follow-up.

The sleep difficulty occurs repeatedly over months.
Sleep effort and clock-watching worsen arousal.
Sleep apnea includes breathing disturbance and nonrestorative sleep.
The case favors Insomnia Disorder because sleep difficulty itself is the central complaint.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 667
clues: A 41-year-old parent presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during a primary care consultation.
The pattern is long-standing and appears across relationships and settings.

Anger, emptiness, and identity disturbance are prominent.
PTSD is trauma-anchored with intrusion and avoidance clusters.
The final clue is abandonment fear, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, and self-harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 668
clues: A 31-year-old entrepreneur has become unusually energetic, irritable, and hard to interrupt during an emergency assessment.

Spending, sexual risk, driving, or business decisions have become reckless.
Irritability escalates when others challenge grand plans.
The symptoms are not better explained by stimulant intoxication or a primary psychotic disorder.
The final clue is mania with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, risk, and marked impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar I Disorder because it describes a manic episode with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, risky behavior, and marked impairment or psychosis.

cases.title: Training Case 669
clues: A 45-year-old business owner has a long pattern of violating others' rights and disregarding consequences during a primary care consultation.

The final clue is pervasive rights violation, deceit, impulsivity, irresponsibility, and lack of remorse.
cases.explanation: The case points to Antisocial Personality Disorder because there is a pervasive adult pattern of violating others' rights, with evidence of conduct problems before adulthood.

cases.title: Training Case 670
clues: A 63-year-old retired nurse reports low energy and trouble keeping up with usual responsibilities during an outpatient visit.
The person can identify a period before the episode when mood, interest, and energy were much better.
The presentation is episodic and represents a clear change from previous functioning.
The person reports feeling worthless and has passive thoughts that life is not worth much, without psychotic symptoms.
Symptoms are not limited to anxiety about one topic, traumatic reminders, or chronic personality traits.
The best-fitting diagnosis centers on a major depressive episode with distress or impairment and no manic history.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 671
clues: A 15-year-old student is referred for chronic disorganization, forgetfulness, and restlessness during a family-requested evaluation.
Inattention includes losing items, careless mistakes, distractibility, and difficulty finishing tasks.
Symptoms began in childhood and appear in more than one setting.
The person struggles with sustained effort even for important tasks.
Autism includes social communication and restricted behavior patterns beyond attention symptoms.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a childhood-onset pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity across settings.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 672
clues: A 67-year-old widower says low mood has felt like the default setting for years during an outpatient visit.
The pattern has persisted for more than two years in adulthood.
There is no history of mania or hypomania.
The person describes being functional but persistently joyless and self-critical.
Symptoms are not better explained by a personality disorder, substance use, or bipolar spectrum condition.
The case favors a persistent depressive pattern rather than a single isolated depressive episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 673
clues: A 27-year-old dancer is seen after marked weight loss and rigid food rules during an outpatient visit.
The person evaluates self-worth heavily through shape, weight, or control.
The problem is not simply low appetite from depression or fear of choking.
Body image is distorted or weight's seriousness is minimized.
Body Dysmorphic Disorder focuses on perceived flaws, not low weight with restriction.
The best-fitting diagnosis is restriction causing significantly low weight with fear of weight gain and body image disturbance.
cases.explanation: The case points to Anorexia Nervosa because restriction leads to significantly low weight, with fear of weight gain and disturbed body image or denial of seriousness.

cases.title: Training Case 674
clues: A 27-year-old veteran avoids reminders of a life-threatening experience during an emergency assessment.
Negative mood, guilt, detachment, or distorted blame has developed after the trauma.
Nightmares, flashbacks, or unwanted memories recur despite efforts to avoid them.
The symptoms are trauma-linked rather than free-floating anxiety.
Panic Disorder does not require trauma-linked intrusions and avoidance.
The final clue is trauma exposure plus re-experiencing, avoidance, negative mood, hyperarousal, and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 675
clues: A 29-year-old collector is referred after rooms became unusable because of saved items during an emergency assessment.
Fire safety, falls, or family conflict have become concerns.
The person believes items may be needed, are sentimental, or must not be wasted.
Rooms cannot be used for intended purposes.
Neurocognitive disorders can cause clutter but usually involve decline and poor organization for different reasons.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent difficulty discarding possessions with clutter and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 676
clues: A 44-year-old surgeon has tried to cut down drinking but repeatedly returns to heavy use during a primary care consultation.
Alcohol is taken in larger amounts or for longer than intended.
Use continues despite interpersonal, occupational, or physical harm.
The person minimizes use despite collateral concern.
Depression or anxiety may coexist but does not explain the impaired control over alcohol.
The final clue is continued alcohol use despite harm plus tolerance or withdrawal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 677
clues: A 27-year-old dancer is seen after marked weight loss and rigid food rules during a family-requested evaluation.
The person may deny the seriousness of low weight.
The person evaluates self-worth heavily through shape, weight, or control.
Fear of gaining weight persists despite medical risk.
Bulimia Nervosa usually has normal or higher weight and recurrent binge-purge cycles.
The case favors Anorexia Nervosa because restriction and low weight are central.
cases.explanation: The case points to Anorexia Nervosa because restriction leads to significantly low weight, with fear of weight gain and disturbed body image or denial of seriousness.

cases.title: Training Case 678
clues: A 29-year-old bartender continues drinking despite arguments, blackouts, and medical concerns during an emergency assessment.
Alcohol is taken in larger amounts or for longer than intended.
Use continues despite interpersonal, occupational, or physical harm.
The person minimizes use despite collateral concern.
Sedative use disorder involves a different substance class.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic pattern of alcohol use with impaired control, impairment, risky use, or pharmacologic features.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 679
clues: A 27-year-old veteran avoids reminders of a life-threatening experience during a telehealth follow-up.
The event involved actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence.

Intrusion, avoidance, negative mood and cognition changes, and arousal symptoms occur together.

cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 680
clues: A 42-year-old project manager reports unfinished tasks, missed deadlines, and difficulty sustaining attention during a primary care consultation.
Symptoms began in childhood and appear in more than one setting.

The person struggles with sustained effort even for important tasks.
Anxiety can impair concentration but is driven by worry.
The case favors ADHD because symptoms are chronic, cross-situational, and began early.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 681
clues: A 15-year-old student has a long history of impulsivity and trouble waiting turns during an emergency assessment.
The symptoms cause school, work, relationship, or safety impairment.
Hyperactivity or impulsivity includes fidgeting, interrupting, impatience, and feeling driven.
Problems are developmental rather than new-onset in adulthood.
Depression causes low motivation and slowed thinking during mood episodes.
The final clue is developmentally persistent attention and impulse-control symptoms with impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 682
clues: A 9-year-old child is referred for chronic disorganization, forgetfulness, and restlessness during a primary care consultation.
Hyperactivity or impulsivity includes fidgeting, interrupting, impatience, and feeling driven.
Teachers, family, or coworkers describe a consistent pattern rather than a recent decline.
External structure helps but does not eliminate the symptoms.
Depression causes low motivation and slowed thinking during mood episodes.
The case favors ADHD because symptoms are chronic, cross-situational, and began early.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 683
clues: A 52-year-old executive repeatedly checks the body and searches online for disease signs during a family-requested evaluation.
The person either seeks repeated care or avoids appointments from fear of bad news.

The person overinterprets normal sensations as evidence of disease.

cases.title: Training Case 684
clues: A 17-year-old student describes feeling slowed down and unable to enjoy previous routines during an emergency assessment.
Symptoms have clustered for more than two weeks rather than appearing as brief mood shifts.
The person can identify a period before the episode when mood, interest, and energy were much better.
Family notes slowed speech, diminished pleasure, and difficulty making decisions.
The key clinical weight is an affective episode with neurovegetative and cognitive changes.
The final clue is a sustained depressive episode with impaired functioning and no substance or bipolar explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 685

Binges occur with rapid eating, eating when not hungry, eating until uncomfortably full, or eating alone from embarrassment.

The final clue is loss-of-control binges with shame and no regular purging or fasting.

cases.title: Training Case 686
clues: A 41-year-old accountant describes feeling slowed down and unable to enjoy previous routines during a telehealth follow-up.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
The presentation is episodic and represents a clear change from previous functioning.
Family notes slowed speech, diminished pleasure, and difficulty making decisions.
Symptoms are not limited to anxiety about one topic, traumatic reminders, or chronic personality traits.
The syndrome is best understood as a unipolar depressive episode rather than a primary anxiety, trauma, or psychotic disorder.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 687
clues: A 22-year-old student presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during an emergency assessment.
Identity, goals, and self-image feel unstable.
Brief stress-related paranoia or dissociation may occur.
Fear of abandonment drives frantic efforts to prevent separation.
Histrionic Personality Disorder is attention-seeking but usually lacks self-harm and abandonment panic.
The final clue is abandonment fear, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, and self-harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 688
clues: A 39-year-old parent has hallucinations and delusions but the duration remains intermediate during a telehealth follow-up.

cases.title: Training Case 689

cases.title: Training Case 690
clues: A 67-year-old retiree continues drinking despite arguments, blackouts, and medical concerns during a primary care consultation.
Risky situations such as driving or operating equipment while intoxicated occur.
Alcohol is taken in larger amounts or for longer than intended.
Morning tremor or sweating improves after alcohol.
Social drinking lacks the persistent impairment and loss of control.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic pattern of alcohol use with impaired control, impairment, risky use, or pharmacologic features.

cases.title: Training Case 691
clues: A 39-year-old teacher has started avoiding activities that might trigger racing heart sensations during a family-requested evaluation.
Avoidance now includes exercise, caffeine, driving, or being alone.
Some attacks occur unexpectedly rather than only in a specific feared situation.
Anticipatory anxiety about future attacks has become disabling.
The key is recurrent unexpected attacks plus persistent concern or maladaptive behavior.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent unexpected panic attacks followed by worry or behavioral change.

cases.title: Training Case 692
clues: A 18-year-old student presents with muscle tension, poor sleep, and difficulty controlling worry during a primary care consultation.
The worry has been present for months and spans health, money, work, family, and small decisions.
There are no recurrent unexpected panic attacks as the main problem.
Somatic tension, sleep disturbance, and keyed-up feelings accompany the worry.
The main symptom is worry, not obsessions, panic surges, or traumatic re-experiencing.
The final clue is difficult-to-control worry more days than not, across multiple topics.

cases.title: Training Case 693
clues: A 24-year-old student reports binges of stimulant use followed by crashes and failed attempts to stop during a student health appointment.

cases.title: Training Case 694

cases.title: Training Case 696
clues: A 52-year-old sales manager has tried to cut down drinking but repeatedly returns to heavy use during an outpatient visit.
Craving, time spent drinking or recovering, and role impairment are present.
Risky situations such as driving or operating equipment while intoxicated occur.
The person minimizes use despite collateral concern.
