cases.title: Training Case 697
clues: A 63-year-old retiree has daytime fatigue and irritability despite adequate chance to sleep during an emergency assessment.
Daytime consequences include fatigue, poor concentration, mood irritability, or work impairment.
Anxiety or depression may coexist but does not fully explain the sleep complaint.
The person may sleep better away from the usual bedroom.
Sleep apnea includes breathing disturbance and nonrestorative sleep.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty with daytime impairment despite adequate sleep opportunity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 698
clues: A 27-year-old dancer reports intense fear of weight gain despite being medically underweight during a student health appointment.
The problem is not simply low appetite from depression or fear of choking.
Body weight is significantly low for age, sex, development, and health context.
Body image is distorted or weight's seriousness is minimized.
Body Dysmorphic Disorder focuses on perceived flaws, not low weight with restriction.
The best-fitting diagnosis is restriction causing significantly low weight with fear of weight gain and body image disturbance.
cases.explanation: The case points to Anorexia Nervosa because restriction leads to significantly low weight, with fear of weight gain and disturbed body image or denial of seriousness.

cases.title: Training Case 699
clues: A 10-year-old child reports lifelong difficulty reading social cues alongside unusually focused interests during an outpatient visit.
The person may mask symptoms but becomes exhausted by social demands.
Social reciprocity, nonverbal communication, and peer relationships are affected.
Conversation is one-sided or literal, and nonverbal cues are hard to interpret.
Social Anxiety Disorder begins around fear of judgment rather than early social communication differences.
The case favors Autism Spectrum Disorder because both social communication and restricted behavior domains are present.
cases.explanation: The case points to Autism Spectrum Disorder because persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors are present from early development and cause functional impact.

cases.title: Training Case 700
clues: A 12-year-old child has restricted eating because of sensory aversion, low interest, or fear of consequences during an emergency assessment.
Medical conditions do not fully explain the restriction.
The pattern is more than ordinary picky eating.
The avoidance is not motivated by pursuit of thinness.
Anorexia Nervosa has weight-gain fear or body image disturbance.
The case favors ARFID because eating restriction is clinically impairing but not anorexia.
cases.explanation: The case points to Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder because restrictive intake causes nutritional or psychosocial impairment without weight or shape disturbance.

cases.title: Training Case 701
clues: A 32-year-old freelancer describes years of alternating emotional highs and lows that never fully meet episode thresholds during a student health appointment.
Symptoms are present much of the time and create relationship and work problems.
The person says the shifts are more chronic than episodic major depression.
At times the person sleeps less, talks more, and takes on projects; at other times motivation and self-esteem drop.
A full hypomanic episode plus major depression would indicate Bipolar II Disorder.
The final clue is years of alternating hypomanic and depressive symptoms without full manic, hypomanic, or major depressive episodes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 702
clues: A 29-year-old bartender continues drinking despite arguments, blackouts, and medical concerns during a student health appointment.
Tolerance or withdrawal symptoms may appear.
Important activities have been reduced or abandoned.
Attempts to stop are short-lived.
Depression or anxiety may coexist but does not explain the impaired control over alcohol.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic pattern of alcohol use with impaired control, impairment, risky use, or pharmacologic features.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 703
clues: A 38-year-old librarian reports never feeling fully well between periods of worse depression during a telehealth follow-up.
There have not been symptom-free stretches longer than a couple of months.
There is no history of mania or hypomania.
The person describes being functional but persistently joyless and self-critical.
Major depressive episodes may occur on top of the chronic pattern, but the long baseline course is the clue.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a chronic depressive disorder lasting years with ongoing impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 704
clues: A 29-year-old collector is referred after rooms became unusable because of saved items during a family-requested evaluation.
The person believes items may be needed, are sentimental, or must not be wasted.
Acquiring items may be excessive but the key problem is discarding.
The person resists cleanup because each item feels potentially important.
OCD rituals may involve saving, but hoarding disorder is defined by discarding difficulty.
The case favors Hoarding Disorder because clutter and saving are the central syndrome.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 705
clues: A 35-year-old programmer has restricted eating because of sensory aversion, low interest, or fear of consequences during a student health appointment.
The person may avoid textures, smells, swallowing sensations, or fear vomiting.
There is no intense fear of weight gain or disturbance in body image.
Food variety is very limited and health or functioning is affected.
Anorexia Nervosa has weight-gain fear or body image disturbance.
The case favors ARFID because eating restriction is clinically impairing but not anorexia.
cases.explanation: The case points to Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder because restrictive intake causes nutritional or psychosocial impairment without weight or shape disturbance.

cases.title: Training Case 706
clues: A 30-year-old delivery driver reports binges of stimulant use followed by crashes and failed attempts to stop during a family-requested evaluation.
Craving and time spent obtaining or recovering from stimulants are prominent.
Sleep and appetite are disrupted.
Binges lead to days without sleep and then exhaustion.
Primary psychotic disorder persists beyond substance effects.
The case favors Stimulant Use Disorder because the symptoms track stimulant use and consequences.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 707
clues: A 37-year-old office worker avoids mirrors and photos because of distress about appearance during an emergency assessment.
The concern is repetitive, intrusive, and hard to resist.
The concern is not primarily about body weight or fatness.
Social avoidance comes from fear that others will notice the perceived defect.
Delusional intensity can occur, but the diagnosis remains BDD when the theme is appearance.
The best-fitting diagnosis is an appearance preoccupation with repetitive behaviors and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 708
clues: A 17-year-old student reports low energy and trouble keeping up with usual responsibilities during a primary care consultation.
Sleep, appetite, concentration, and self-worth have changed in the same period.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
Most days include depressed mood or marked loss of interest, plus guilt, fatigue, and poor concentration.
The episode is more time-limited and symptom-dense than a lifelong low mood pattern.
The syndrome is best understood as a unipolar depressive episode rather than a primary anxiety, trauma, or psychotic disorder.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 709
clues: A 23-year-old student is evaluated after recent onset of disorganized speech and suspicious beliefs during a family-requested evaluation.
Mood episodes do not dominate the illness.
The symptom pattern resembles schizophrenia in active-phase features.
The person may be too early in the course to determine whether schizophrenia will develop.
Schizoaffective Disorder requires a specific mood-psychosis relationship.
The final clue is active psychotic symptoms lasting more than a month but not yet six months.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophreniform Disorder because schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms have lasted longer than brief psychosis but have not reached the longer schizophrenia duration threshold.

cases.title: Training Case 710
clues: A 34-year-old designer presents with a current depressive episode but mentions bursts of unusually high productivity during a family-requested evaluation.
The elevated periods last several days and are noticeable to others.
Friends describe the person as more talkative, social, impulsive, and distractible during these spells.
The hypomanic periods are clear departures from baseline but do not become full mania.
Antidepressant activation should be distinguished from spontaneous hypomania.
The case favors Bipolar II Disorder because the elevated mood never reaches full manic severity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar II Disorder because it combines major depressive episodes with hypomanic periods that are observable and episodic but not severe enough to qualify as mania.

cases.title: Training Case 711
clues: A 52-year-old executive is preoccupied with cancer, neurologic disease, or heart disease despite reassurance during a telehealth follow-up.
Medical evaluation has not supported the feared illness.
The preoccupation has lasted months and reassurance fades quickly.
Functional impairment comes from disease preoccupation.
OCD has broader intrusive thoughts and rituals.
The final clue is disease preoccupation, checking, and reassurance cycles despite little somatic burden.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 712
clues: A 8-year-old child avoids many foods after choking, nausea, or sensory distress during a student health appointment.
The person may avoid textures, smells, swallowing sensations, or fear vomiting.
The restriction causes weight loss, nutritional deficiency, supplement dependence, or psychosocial impairment.
The avoidance is not motivated by pursuit of thinness.
Autism may coexist, but ARFID describes the feeding disturbance itself.
The case favors ARFID because eating restriction is clinically impairing but not anorexia.
cases.explanation: The case points to Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder because restrictive intake causes nutritional or psychosocial impairment without weight or shape disturbance.

cases.title: Training Case 713
clues: A 35-year-old parent repeatedly checks the body and searches online for disease signs during an emergency assessment.
The preoccupation has lasted months and reassurance fades quickly.
The concern is not better explained by delusional disorder or OCD.
The person overinterprets normal sensations as evidence of disease.
Generalized Anxiety Disorder includes many worry domains rather than illness preoccupation.
The case favors Illness Anxiety Disorder because the feared illness is central.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 714
clues: A 36-year-old writer reports voices and paranoia along with episodes of mania or depression during an outpatient visit.
Substance use does not explain the psychotic course.
There has been a period of psychosis lasting at least two weeks without a major mood episode.
Delusions or hallucinations persist outside mood episodes.
Bipolar I with psychotic features does not have extended psychosis outside mania or depression.
The best-fitting diagnosis is psychosis outside mood episodes plus mood episodes present for much of the illness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 715
clues: A 29-year-old collector feels intense distress when family tries to discard possessions during a family-requested evaluation.
Fire safety, falls, or family conflict have become concerns.
The difficulty discarding has grown over years.
Discarding even low-value objects causes marked distress.
OCD rituals may involve saving, but hoarding disorder is defined by discarding difficulty.
The case favors Hoarding Disorder because clutter and saving are the central syndrome.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 716
clues: A 36-year-old engineer says the mind rarely turns off even when problems are minor during a telehealth follow-up.
There are no recurrent unexpected panic attacks as the main problem.
Reassurance helps briefly but the worry quickly shifts to another topic.
Avoidance and reassurance-seeking are spreading across daily life.
The time course distinguishes it from brief stress reactions.
The final clue is difficult-to-control worry more days than not, across multiple topics.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 717
clues: A 35-year-old parent arrives late because rituals take hours each morning during a telehealth follow-up.
The rituals temporarily reduce anxiety but the relief fades quickly.
Insight may vary, but the person is distressed by the cycle.
Obsessions include contamination, harm, symmetry, taboo thoughts, or fear of mistakes.
The time-consuming ritual cycle is the diagnostic center.
The best-fitting diagnosis is obsessions plus compulsions that are distressing, time-consuming, or impairing.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 718
clues: A 30-year-old lab technician develops psychotic symptoms over several weeks with uncertain long-term course during a primary care consultation.
The symptom pattern resembles schizophrenia in active-phase features.
There is no clear substance, medication, or medical cause.
Symptoms persist beyond a brief psychotic episode.
Schizophrenia requires a longer illness duration.
The final clue is active psychotic symptoms lasting more than a month but not yet six months.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophreniform Disorder because schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms have lasted longer than brief psychosis but have not reached the longer schizophrenia duration threshold.

cases.title: Training Case 719
clues: A 43-year-old neighbor has a persistent persecutory, jealous, grandiose, somatic, or erotomanic belief during a family-requested evaluation.
Substances and medical causes do not explain the belief.
Functioning outside the delusional topic is relatively preserved.
The belief is fixed despite contrary evidence.
OCD can have poor insight but thoughts are often intrusive and ritual-linked.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a persistent delusion with otherwise relatively preserved functioning.
cases.explanation: The case points to Delusional Disorder because a fixed delusional belief persists while broader schizophrenia-spectrum features and marked functional decline are absent.

cases.title: Training Case 720
clues: A 72-year-old retiree continues to feel life is frozen after the death of a loved one during an emergency assessment.
The person avoids reminders yet also searches for closeness to the deceased.
The person may feel identity disruption, disbelief, bitterness, or meaninglessness tied to the death.
The person denies a wish to die but says life feels empty without the deceased.
Major depression is broader and not necessarily centered on separation distress.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent, impairing grief with yearning and preoccupation long after the death.
cases.explanation: The case points to Prolonged Grief Disorder because persistent yearning and preoccupation after bereavement remain intense, impairing, and grief-specific well beyond the acute mourning period.

cases.title: Training Case 721
clues: A 18-year-old student presents with muscle tension, poor sleep, and difficulty controlling worry during a family-requested evaluation.
The worry has been present for months and spans health, money, work, family, and small decisions.
The anxiety is not limited to scrutiny, contamination, trauma reminders, or a single phobic object.
Somatic tension, sleep disturbance, and keyed-up feelings accompany the worry.
The time course distinguishes it from brief stress reactions.
The final clue is difficult-to-control worry more days than not, across multiple topics.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 722
clues: A 57-year-old driver has started avoiding activities that might trigger racing heart sensations during a family-requested evaluation.
Cardiac and endocrine evaluations do not explain the episodes.
Some attacks occur unexpectedly rather than only in a specific feared situation.
The attacks are abrupt, intense, and time-limited.
The key is recurrent unexpected attacks plus persistent concern or maladaptive behavior.
The case is not simply stress because the attacks are abrupt, recurrent, and followed by avoidance.
cases.explanation: The case points to Panic Disorder because recurrent unexpected panic attacks are followed by persistent concern about attacks and avoidance of internal cues or situations.

cases.title: Training Case 723
clues: A 55-year-old technician reports binges of stimulant use followed by crashes and failed attempts to stop during a family-requested evaluation.
Sleep and appetite are disrupted.
Tolerance, risky use, and continued use despite harm may be present.
Use continues despite medical danger or psychotic symptoms.
ADHD treatment taken as prescribed is not stimulant use disorder.
The case favors Stimulant Use Disorder because the symptoms track stimulant use and consequences.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 724
clues: A 50-year-old train operator avoids reminders of a life-threatening experience during a primary care consultation.
Negative mood, guilt, detachment, or distorted blame has developed after the trauma.
The person avoids places, people, conversations, or sensations linked to the trauma.
The symptoms are trauma-linked rather than free-floating anxiety.
Adjustment Disorder lacks the full trauma symptom clusters.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a trauma-related syndrome lasting more than a month with intrusion, avoidance, mood changes, and arousal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 725
clues: A 31-year-old nurse spends hours monitoring bodily sensations and seeking reassurance during a student health appointment.
The symptoms are not intentionally produced.
The person repeatedly researches symptoms and seeks consultations.
Reassurance is short-lived.
Panic Disorder involves abrupt attacks rather than persistent symptom preoccupation.
The final clue is disproportionate health-related preoccupation around actual symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Somatic Symptom Disorder because distressing physical symptoms are accompanied by excessive health-related thoughts, anxiety, and behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 726
clues: A 32-year-old assault survivor reports sleep disturbance and irritability after a traumatic event during a family-requested evaluation.
The event involved actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence.
Symptoms have lasted more than a month and are impairing work or relationships.
The symptoms are trauma-linked rather than free-floating anxiety.
Adjustment Disorder lacks the full trauma symptom clusters.
The final clue is trauma exposure plus re-experiencing, avoidance, negative mood, hyperarousal, and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 727
clues: A 41-year-old accountant reports low energy and trouble keeping up with usual responsibilities during a family-requested evaluation.
Alcohol, stimulants, bereavement alone, and thyroid disease do not better explain the syndrome.
Symptoms have clustered for more than two weeks rather than appearing as brief mood shifts.
Family notes slowed speech, diminished pleasure, and difficulty making decisions.
The episode is more time-limited and symptom-dense than a lifelong low mood pattern.
The best-fitting diagnosis centers on a major depressive episode with distress or impairment and no manic history.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 728
clues: A 37-year-old office worker seeks repeated reassurance and cosmetic opinions about one body area during a student health appointment.
The concern is repetitive, intrusive, and hard to resist.
The concern is not primarily about body weight or fatness.
The preoccupation is narrow and appearance-focused.
Delusional intensity can occur, but the diagnosis remains BDD when the theme is appearance.
The case favors Body Dysmorphic Disorder rather than social anxiety because appearance preoccupation drives the avoidance.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 729
clues: A 35-year-old parent fears having a serious illness despite minimal symptoms during a student health appointment.
Somatic symptoms are absent or mild.
Medical evaluation has not supported the feared illness.
The person overinterprets normal sensations as evidence of disease.
Somatic Symptom Disorder has prominent distressing symptoms.
The case favors Illness Anxiety Disorder because the feared illness is central.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 730
clues: A 52-year-old office worker seeks help because one fixed belief has dominated life for months during a primary care consultation.
The belief has persisted for at least a month.
There is no broad disorganized speech, negative symptoms, or marked deterioration.
The person acts on the belief through complaints, investigations, or confrontations.
OCD can have poor insight but thoughts are often intrusive and ritual-linked.
The final clue is fixed delusional belief without schizophrenia-like disorganization or negative symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Delusional Disorder because a fixed delusional belief persists while broader schizophrenia-spectrum features and marked functional decline are absent.

cases.title: Training Case 731
clues: A 30-year-old attorney reports intense fear of being judged during conversations or performance during a family-requested evaluation.
Panic symptoms can occur, but they are tied to social evaluation.
The person worries about blushing, shaking, sounding foolish, or being rejected.
The person uses safety behaviors such as rehearsing, avoiding eye contact, or speaking as little as possible.
Symptoms are not simply introversion because they cause distress and impairment.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent fear of social evaluation with avoidance and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Social Anxiety Disorder because anxiety and avoidance center on possible negative evaluation in social or performance situations, causing impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 732
clues: A 27-year-old resident physician describes depressive lows interrupted by briefer periods of increased confidence and energy during a telehealth follow-up.
The elevated periods do not cause hospitalization or psychosis.
The person remembers starting multiple projects and spending more than usual.
There is a history of major depressive episodes.
A single past manic episode would change the answer to Bipolar I Disorder.
The case favors Bipolar II Disorder because the elevated mood never reaches full manic severity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar II Disorder because it combines major depressive episodes with hypomanic periods that are observable and episodic but not severe enough to qualify as mania.

cases.title: Training Case 733
clues: A 29-year-old bartender continues drinking despite arguments, blackouts, and medical concerns during a student health appointment.
Alcohol is taken in larger amounts or for longer than intended.
Important activities have been reduced or abandoned.
Attempts to stop are short-lived.
Depression or anxiety may coexist but does not explain the impaired control over alcohol.
The case favors Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol-related control and consequences dominate.
cases.explanation: The case points to Alcohol Use Disorder because alcohol use shows impaired control, social impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and tolerance or withdrawal features.

cases.title: Training Case 734
clues: A 35-year-old server presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during an outpatient visit.
Mood shifts are rapid and reactive, often lasting hours rather than discrete mood episodes.
Recurrent self-harm, suicidal gestures, or threats occur during crises.
Fear of abandonment drives frantic efforts to prevent separation.
Histrionic Personality Disorder is attention-seeking but usually lacks self-harm and abandonment panic.
The final clue is abandonment fear, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, and self-harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 735
clues: A 28-year-old graduate student reports withdrawal symptoms that improve after opioid use during an emergency assessment.
Use has escalated and control has diminished.
Craving remains strong despite serious consequences.
Important activities are reduced around use.
Pain disorder alone does not explain craving and compulsive use.
The case favors Opioid Use Disorder because opioid-related impairment and loss of control are central.
cases.explanation: The case points to Opioid Use Disorder because opioid use is marked by impaired control, craving, role impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and withdrawal or tolerance features.

cases.title: Training Case 736
clues: A 65-year-old retiree seeks help because one fixed belief has dominated life for months during a family-requested evaluation.

Substances and medical causes do not explain the belief.
The belief is fixed despite contrary evidence.
Body Dysmorphic Disorder is used when the fixed belief is appearance-focused.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a persistent delusion with otherwise relatively preserved functioning.
cases.explanation: The case points to Delusional Disorder because a fixed delusional belief persists while broader schizophrenia-spectrum features and marked functional decline are absent.

cases.title: Training Case 737
clues: A 22-year-old student reports secret episodes of eating far more than intended followed by compensatory behavior during an outpatient visit.
Self-evaluation is strongly influenced by weight and shape.
Medical complications such as electrolyte problems or parotid swelling may appear.
The key cycle is binge eating followed by behaviors meant to prevent weight gain.
Binge-Eating Disorder lacks recurrent compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 738

cases.title: Training Case 739

The person describes loss of control rather than ordinary overeating.
The person feels disgusted, depressed, or guilty afterward.
The binge episodes are recurrent over time.

The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent distressing binge eating without compensatory behavior.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 741
clues: A 44-year-old parent says the mind rarely turns off even when problems are minor during an emergency assessment.
The anxiety is not limited to scrutiny, contamination, trauma reminders, or a single phobic object.
The person feels restless, fatigued, irritable, and unable to concentrate.
Avoidance and reassurance-seeking are spreading across daily life.
The breadth of worry distinguishes it from phobias and social anxiety.

cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 742

Avoidance now includes exercise, caffeine, driving, or being alone.

The key is recurrent unexpected attacks plus persistent concern or maladaptive behavior.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent unexpected panic attacks followed by worry or behavioral change.

cases.title: Training Case 743
clues: A 27-year-old dancer reports intense fear of weight gain despite being medically underweight during a telehealth follow-up.
The person may deny the seriousness of low weight.
The person evaluates self-worth heavily through shape, weight, or control.

ARFID lacks fear of weight gain or body image disturbance.
The best-fitting diagnosis is restriction causing significantly low weight with fear of weight gain and body image disturbance.

The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic stimulant use pattern with craving, impairment, risky use, and continued use despite harm.

cases.title: Training Case 745
clues: A 24-year-old model spends hours focused on a perceived flaw others barely notice during a primary care consultation.
The concern is not primarily about body weight or fatness.
Work, school, dating, or leaving home is impaired.
The preoccupation is narrow and appearance-focused.
OCD can include appearance obsessions, but BDD is specifically about perceived defects.
The case favors Body Dysmorphic Disorder rather than social anxiety because appearance preoccupation drives the avoidance.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 746
clues: A 48-year-old pharmacist arrives late because rituals take hours each morning during a telehealth follow-up.
The rituals temporarily reduce anxiety but the relief fades quickly.
The concern is not limited to appearance, hoarding, illness anxiety, or ordinary worry.
Compulsions include checking, washing, counting, ordering, reassurance-seeking, or mental reviewing.
Delusions are fixed beliefs; OCD thoughts are intrusive and repetitive even when insight is poor.
The final clue is unwanted intrusive thoughts and repetitive rituals aimed at neutralizing anxiety.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 747
clues: A 48-year-old pharmacist arrives late because rituals take hours each morning during a telehealth follow-up.
There is no substance or psychotic disorder that better explains the repetitive behavior.
The pattern consumes significant time and interferes with school, work, or relationships.
Compulsions include checking, washing, counting, ordering, reassurance-seeking, or mental reviewing.
Delusions are fixed beliefs; OCD thoughts are intrusive and repetitive even when insight is poor.

cases.title: Training Case 748

cases.title: Training Case 750
clues: A 47-year-old mechanic has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during a telehealth follow-up.

cases.title: Training Case 797
clues: A 41-year-old parent presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during a student health appointment.
Mood shifts are rapid and reactive, often lasting hours rather than discrete mood episodes.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
Fear of abandonment drives frantic efforts to prevent separation.
Histrionic Personality Disorder is attention-seeking but usually lacks self-harm and abandonment panic.
The case favors Borderline Personality Disorder because the pattern is trait-like and interpersonal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 798
clues: A 41-year-old office assistant has become socially withdrawn and increasingly suspicious during an emergency assessment.
Negative symptoms such as reduced speech, low motivation, or flat affect are present.
Hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech, or disorganized behavior are prominent.
The psychotic symptoms persist outside mood episodes.
Schizoaffective Disorder requires a substantial mood component plus psychosis outside mood episodes.
The final clue is psychosis lasting months with deterioration and symptoms not restricted to mood episodes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophrenia because psychotic symptoms, negative symptoms, functional decline, and a prolonged course occur outside mood episodes and without a better substance or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 799
clues: A 24-year-old model spends hours focused on a perceived flaw others barely notice during a primary care consultation.
Medical examination does not match the intensity of the concern.
Checking, grooming, comparing, camouflaging, or reassurance-seeking takes substantial time.
The person repeatedly checks mirrors, searches online procedures, or compares features with others.
Eating disorders center on weight or shape concerns with eating behavior disturbance.
The final clue is distress over a perceived physical flaw with checking, comparing, and camouflaging.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 800
clues: A 22-year-old student has dental enamel erosion, shame, and repeated attempts to stop purging during an emergency assessment.
The pattern recurs over time and is not limited to episodes of anorexia nervosa.
Binge episodes involve loss of control and unusually large intake.
The person feels unable to control the binge-purge cycle.
Purging Disorder has purging without objectively large binges.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent binge eating with compensatory behaviors and shape-weight overvaluation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bulimia Nervosa because recurrent binge eating with loss of control is followed by compensatory behaviors, with self-evaluation tied to weight or shape.

cases.title: Training Case 801
clues: A 51-year-old trainer avoids mirrors and photos because of distress about appearance during a primary care consultation.
The concern is not primarily about body weight or fatness.
Checking, grooming, comparing, camouflaging, or reassurance-seeking takes substantial time.
Social avoidance comes from fear that others will notice the perceived defect.
Delusional intensity can occur, but the diagnosis remains BDD when the theme is appearance.
The case favors Body Dysmorphic Disorder rather than social anxiety because appearance preoccupation drives the avoidance.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 802
clues: A 29-year-old software analyst is evaluated for long-standing social communication differences during a telehealth follow-up.
Social reciprocity, nonverbal communication, and peer relationships are affected.
The presentation is not explained only by social fear or attention problems.
Interests are intense, specific, and absorbing.
ADHD causes impulsivity and inattention but does not fully explain restricted interests and reciprocity differences.
The best-fitting diagnosis is early-emerging social communication differences plus restricted or repetitive patterns.
cases.explanation: The case points to Autism Spectrum Disorder because persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors are present from early development and cause functional impact.

cases.title: Training Case 803
clues: A 36-year-old writer has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during an emergency assessment.
The longitudinal pattern is more than mood disorder with psychotic features.
The person has had syndromal mania or depression plus schizophrenia-spectrum symptoms.
Mood episodes are prominent across the overall illness.
Bipolar I with psychotic features does not have extended psychosis outside mania or depression.
The best-fitting diagnosis is psychosis outside mood episodes plus mood episodes present for much of the illness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 804
clues: A 28-year-old artist has become socially withdrawn and increasingly suspicious during a telehealth follow-up.
Functioning at school, work, or relationships is clearly below previous levels.
Hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech, or disorganized behavior are prominent.
Family reports deterioration in hygiene, social engagement, and goal-directed behavior.
Schizoaffective Disorder requires a substantial mood component plus psychosis outside mood episodes.
The best-fitting diagnosis is chronic psychosis with functional decline and negative symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophrenia because psychotic symptoms, negative symptoms, functional decline, and a prolonged course occur outside mood episodes and without a better substance or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 805
clues: A 22-year-old graduate student says low mood has felt like the default setting for years during an outpatient visit.
The person continues working but describes constant low energy, poor sleep, and hopelessness.
Symptoms are less explosive than an acute episode but have become woven into daily life.
The chronic mood state is accompanied by low appetite or overeating, poor sleep, fatigue, low self-esteem, and impaired concentration.
Major depressive episodes may occur on top of the chronic pattern, but the long baseline course is the clue.
The case favors a persistent depressive pattern rather than a single isolated depressive episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 806
clues: A 15-year-old student reports unfinished tasks, missed deadlines, and difficulty sustaining attention during a family-requested evaluation.
Symptoms began in childhood and appear in more than one setting.
Mood, anxiety, sleep, substance, and learning problems do not fully explain the pattern.
Problems are developmental rather than new-onset in adulthood.
Anxiety can impair concentration but is driven by worry.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a childhood-onset pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity across settings.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 807
clues: A 40-year-old office manager feels shame after secret eating episodes but does not purge during a student health appointment.
The person describes loss of control rather than ordinary overeating.
Binges occur with rapid eating, eating when not hungry, eating until uncomfortably full, or eating alone from embarrassment.
Distress about binge eating is clinically significant.
Night Eating Syndrome has a circadian eating pattern rather than discrete loss-of-control binges.
The final clue is loss-of-control binges with shame and no regular purging or fasting.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 808
clues: A 44-year-old parent presents with muscle tension, poor sleep, and difficulty controlling worry during an emergency assessment.
The anxiety is not limited to scrutiny, contamination, trauma reminders, or a single phobic object.
The worry has been present for months and spans health, money, work, family, and small decisions.
The person describes excessive worry that is hard to control.
The time course distinguishes it from brief stress reactions.
The final clue is difficult-to-control worry more days than not, across multiple topics.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 809
clues: A 30-year-old lab technician develops psychotic symptoms over several weeks with uncertain long-term course during a student health appointment.
Mood episodes do not dominate the illness.
The disturbance has lasted more than a month but less than six months.
Delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms are present.
Schizoaffective Disorder requires a specific mood-psychosis relationship.
The best-fitting diagnosis is schizophrenia-like psychosis in the one-to-six-month duration window.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophreniform Disorder because schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms have lasted longer than brief psychosis but have not reached the longer schizophrenia duration threshold.

cases.title: Training Case 810
clues: A 32-year-old freelancer describes years of alternating emotional highs and lows that never fully meet episode thresholds during an outpatient visit.
The person says the shifts are more chronic than episodic major depression.
The pattern has lasted for years with many periods of hypomanic symptoms and depressive symptoms.
At times the person sleeps less, talks more, and takes on projects; at other times motivation and self-esteem drop.
A full hypomanic episode plus major depression would indicate Bipolar II Disorder.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a chronic bipolar-spectrum pattern below full mood episode thresholds.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 811
clues: A 43-year-old nurse reports withdrawal symptoms that improve after opioid use during a telehealth follow-up.
Use has escalated and control has diminished.
The person uses in risky settings or combines opioids with sedatives.
Withdrawal includes aches, diarrhea, rhinorrhea, insomnia, anxiety, and cravings.
Pain disorder alone does not explain craving and compulsive use.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a problematic opioid use pattern with impaired control, harm, craving, and possible withdrawal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Opioid Use Disorder because opioid use is marked by impaired control, craving, role impairment, risky use, continued use despite harm, and withdrawal or tolerance features.

cases.title: Training Case 812
clues: A 64-year-old retiree repeatedly checks the body and searches online for disease signs during a student health appointment.
The concern is not better explained by delusional disorder or OCD.
Somatic symptoms are absent or mild.
The person overinterprets normal sensations as evidence of disease.
Somatic Symptom Disorder has prominent distressing symptoms.
The final clue is disease preoccupation, checking, and reassurance cycles despite little somatic burden.
cases.explanation: The case points to Illness Anxiety Disorder because the person is preoccupied with having or acquiring a serious illness despite minimal symptoms and reassurance.

cases.title: Training Case 813
clues: A 24-year-old model seeks repeated reassurance and cosmetic opinions about one body area during a student health appointment.
Work, school, dating, or leaving home is impaired.
Medical examination does not match the intensity of the concern.
The preoccupation is narrow and appearance-focused.
OCD can include appearance obsessions, but BDD is specifically about perceived defects.
The case favors Body Dysmorphic Disorder rather than social anxiety because appearance preoccupation drives the avoidance.
cases.explanation: The case points to Body Dysmorphic Disorder because the person is preoccupied with perceived appearance defects and performs repetitive checking, comparing, or camouflaging behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 814
clues: A 26-year-old medical student reports repetitive checking, washing, or mental rituals during a family-requested evaluation.
The thoughts are ego-dystonic or resisted rather than welcomed.
There is no substance or psychotic disorder that better explains the repetitive behavior.
Obsessions include contamination, harm, symmetry, taboo thoughts, or fear of mistakes.
Delusions are fixed beliefs; OCD thoughts are intrusive and repetitive even when insight is poor.
The final clue is unwanted intrusive thoughts and repetitive rituals aimed at neutralizing anxiety.
cases.explanation: The case points to Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder because intrusive obsessions lead to repetitive compulsions or mental acts that are time-consuming and impairing.

cases.title: Training Case 815
clues: A 18-year-old gymnast reports secret episodes of eating far more than intended followed by compensatory behavior during an emergency assessment.
The pattern recurs over time and is not limited to episodes of anorexia nervosa.
Compensatory behaviors include vomiting, laxatives, fasting, or excessive exercise.
The person feels unable to control the binge-purge cycle.
Binge-Eating Disorder lacks recurrent compensatory behaviors.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent binge eating with compensatory behaviors and shape-weight overvaluation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bulimia Nervosa because recurrent binge eating with loss of control is followed by compensatory behaviors, with self-evaluation tied to weight or shape.

cases.title: Training Case 816
clues: A 47-year-old sibling continues to feel life is frozen after the death of a loved one during an outpatient visit.
Work, relationships, and future planning remain impaired.
The person may feel identity disruption, disbelief, bitterness, or meaninglessness tied to the death.
There is difficulty accepting the death and moving into future roles.
Adjustment Disorder is less persistent and less grief-specific.
The final clue is prolonged separation distress, identity disruption, and functional impairment after bereavement.
cases.explanation: The case points to Prolonged Grief Disorder because persistent yearning and preoccupation after bereavement remain intense, impairing, and grief-specific well beyond the acute mourning period.

cases.title: Training Case 817
clues: A 38-year-old librarian reports never feeling fully well between periods of worse depression during a primary care consultation.
The pattern has persisted for more than two years in adulthood.
Friends say the mood problem predates the latest stressor by years.
The mood problem is long-term rather than confined to a recent two-week episode.
Major depressive episodes may occur on top of the chronic pattern, but the long baseline course is the clue.
The final clue is depressed mood most days across years, with low self-esteem, sleep problems, and hopelessness.
cases.explanation: The case points to Persistent Depressive Disorder because depressed mood and associated symptoms have persisted most of the time for years, with few or no sustained symptom-free intervals.

cases.title: Training Case 818
clues: A 12-year-old child avoids many foods after choking, nausea, or sensory distress during a telehealth follow-up.
Mealtimes create family stress and social avoidance.
The pattern is more than ordinary picky eating.
Food variety is very limited and health or functioning is affected.
Autism may coexist, but ARFID describes the feeding disturbance itself.
The best-fitting diagnosis is restrictive intake with nutritional or functional consequences and no body image driver.
cases.explanation: The case points to Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder because restrictive intake causes nutritional or psychosocial impairment without weight or shape disturbance.

cases.title: Training Case 819
clues: A 15-year-old student is referred for chronic disorganization, forgetfulness, and restlessness during a student health appointment.
Teachers, family, or coworkers describe a consistent pattern rather than a recent decline.
Hyperactivity or impulsivity includes fidgeting, interrupting, impatience, and feeling driven.
Problems are developmental rather than new-onset in adulthood.
Depression causes low motivation and slowed thinking during mood episodes.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a childhood-onset pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity across settings.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 820
clues: A 17-year-old student is brought in after weeks of social withdrawal and missed obligations during a family-requested evaluation.
Symptoms have clustered for more than two weeks rather than appearing as brief mood shifts.
There is no history of a manic or hypomanic episode.
The person reports feeling worthless and has passive thoughts that life is not worth much, without psychotic symptoms.
Symptoms are not limited to anxiety about one topic, traumatic reminders, or chronic personality traits.
The syndrome is best understood as a unipolar depressive episode rather than a primary anxiety, trauma, or psychotic disorder.
cases.explanation: The case points to Major Depressive Disorder because it describes a sustained depressive episode with mood or interest change, neurovegetative and cognitive symptoms, impairment, and no better manic, substance, or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 821
clues: A 35-year-old technician seeks help because one fixed belief has dominated life for months during an emergency assessment.
The belief has persisted for at least a month.
Mood episodes are absent or brief relative to the delusional period.
The belief is fixed despite contrary evidence.
OCD can have poor insight but thoughts are often intrusive and ritual-linked.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a persistent delusion with otherwise relatively preserved functioning.
cases.explanation: The case points to Delusional Disorder because a fixed delusional belief persists while broader schizophrenia-spectrum features and marked functional decline are absent.

cases.title: Training Case 822
clues: A 19-year-old college student is brought in after several nights with almost no sleep and escalating plans during a family-requested evaluation.
The change has lasted long enough to disrupt work, family, and safety.
Psychotic intensity or hospitalization has become part of the episode.
Ideas shift quickly, speech is pressured, and judgment is markedly impaired.
A major depressive episode is common but is not required for the diagnosis.
The final clue is mania with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, risk, and marked impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar I Disorder because it describes a manic episode with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, risky behavior, and marked impairment or psychosis.

cases.title: Training Case 823
clues: A 43-year-old mechanic feels intense distress when family tries to discard possessions during an outpatient visit.
Acquiring items may be excessive but the key problem is discarding.
The difficulty discarding has grown over years.
The person resists cleanup because each item feels potentially important.
Collectors organize and display items; hoarding creates clutter and impairment.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent difficulty discarding possessions with clutter and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 824
clues: A 36-year-old parent reports intense yearning and daily preoccupation with a person who died during an outpatient visit.
The dominant emotion is yearning, longing, or preoccupation rather than generalized low mood alone.
The person avoids reminders yet also searches for closeness to the deceased.
There is difficulty accepting the death and moving into future roles.
PTSD centers on threat re-experiencing rather than yearning for the deceased.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent, impairing grief with yearning and preoccupation long after the death.
cases.explanation: The case points to Prolonged Grief Disorder because persistent yearning and preoccupation after bereavement remain intense, impairing, and grief-specific well beyond the acute mourning period.

cases.title: Training Case 825
clues: A 10-year-old child has difficulty with reciprocal conversation and intense distress with routine changes during an outpatient visit.
Restricted or repetitive behaviors, sensory differences, routines, or highly focused interests are present.
Language level may vary, but social communication differences are persistent.
Interests are intense, specific, and absorbing.
Social Anxiety Disorder begins around fear of judgment rather than early social communication differences.
The case favors Autism Spectrum Disorder because both social communication and restricted behavior domains are present.
cases.explanation: The case points to Autism Spectrum Disorder because persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors are present from early development and cause functional impact.

cases.title: Training Case 826
clues: A 28-year-old chef seeks help because mood instability has become a long-term pattern during a student health appointment.
There are no psychotic symptoms and no history of hospitalization for mood elevation.
Symptoms are present much of the time and create relationship and work problems.
The highs and lows are subthreshold but recurrent.
A full manic episode would indicate Bipolar I Disorder.
The case is tougher because it sits below the threshold for Bipolar I or II.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 827
clues: A 29-year-old graduate student has a history of psychosis and major mood episodes that overlap in complicated ways during a student health appointment.
Major mood episodes are also present for a large portion of the illness.
Substance use does not explain the psychotic course.
Mood episodes are prominent across the overall illness.
Mood disorder with psychotic features has psychosis only during mood episodes.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 828
clues: A 22-year-old student reports chronic emptiness, abandonment panic, and impulsive crises during a primary care consultation.
Recurrent self-harm, suicidal gestures, or threats occur during crises.
The pattern is long-standing and appears across relationships and settings.
Fear of abandonment drives frantic efforts to prevent separation.
Histrionic Personality Disorder is attention-seeking but usually lacks self-harm and abandonment panic.
The final clue is abandonment fear, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, and self-harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 829
clues: A 9-year-old child is referred for chronic disorganization, forgetfulness, and restlessness during a telehealth follow-up.
Symptoms began in childhood and appear in more than one setting.
Inattention includes losing items, careless mistakes, distractibility, and difficulty finishing tasks.
The person struggles with sustained effort even for important tasks.
Autism includes social communication and restricted behavior patterns beyond attention symptoms.
The case favors ADHD because symptoms are chronic, cross-situational, and began early.
cases.explanation: The case points to ADHD because inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity began in childhood, occur across settings, and impair functioning.

cases.title: Training Case 830
clues: A 29-year-old software analyst reports lifelong difficulty reading social cues alongside unusually focused interests during an emergency assessment.
Features were present in early development, even if recognized later.
The person may mask symptoms but becomes exhausted by social demands.
Conversation is one-sided or literal, and nonverbal cues are hard to interpret.
Social Anxiety Disorder begins around fear of judgment rather than early social communication differences.
The best-fitting diagnosis is early-emerging social communication differences plus restricted or repetitive patterns.
cases.explanation: The case points to Autism Spectrum Disorder because persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors are present from early development and cause functional impact.

cases.title: Training Case 831
clues: A 35-year-old parent has daytime fatigue and irritability despite adequate chance to sleep during an emergency assessment.
Anxiety or depression may coexist but does not fully explain the sleep complaint.
Daytime consequences include fatigue, poor concentration, mood irritability, or work impairment.
The person may sleep better away from the usual bedroom.
Circadian rhythm disorders involve misalignment of sleep timing.
The case favors Insomnia Disorder because sleep difficulty itself is the central complaint.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 832
clues: A 45-year-old business owner has a long pattern of violating others' rights and disregarding consequences during an outpatient visit.
Collateral history describes school fights, theft, truancy, cruelty, or arrests before age 15.
There is repeated lying, impulsivity, irritability, reckless disregard, irresponsibility, or lack of remorse.
Risk-taking continues despite danger to self or others.
Narcissistic Personality Disorder may exploit others but does not require conduct-disorder history.
The final clue is pervasive rights violation, deceit, impulsivity, irresponsibility, and lack of remorse.
cases.explanation: The case points to Antisocial Personality Disorder because there is a pervasive adult pattern of violating others' rights, with evidence of conduct problems before adulthood.

cases.title: Training Case 833
clues: A 35-year-old server presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during an emergency assessment.
Identity, goals, and self-image feel unstable.
Brief stress-related paranoia or dissociation may occur.
Fear of abandonment drives frantic efforts to prevent separation.
PTSD is trauma-anchored with intrusion and avoidance clusters.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a pervasive pattern of instability in relationships, self-image, affect, and impulse control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 834
clues: A 52-year-old school administrator reports constant worry that moves from one topic to another during a primary care consultation.
The anxiety is not limited to scrutiny, contamination, trauma reminders, or a single phobic object.
The worry has been present for months and spans health, money, work, family, and small decisions.
Somatic tension, sleep disturbance, and keyed-up feelings accompany the worry.
The breadth of worry distinguishes it from phobias and social anxiety.
The best-fitting diagnosis is chronic excessive worry across many domains with physical tension and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 835
clues: A 29-year-old graduate student reports voices and paranoia along with episodes of mania or depression during a family-requested evaluation.
During mood episodes, psychosis intensifies, but it does not disappear completely between them.
Major mood episodes are also present for a large portion of the illness.
Functional decline and disorganization may appear during psychotic periods.
Mood disorder with psychotic features has psychosis only during mood episodes.
The final clue is the two-part relationship: independent psychosis and substantial mood syndromes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizoaffective Disorder because psychosis occurs for a period without mood episodes, while major mood episodes are also present for much of the illness.
differentials: Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

cases.title: Training Case 836
clues: A 19-year-old college student arrives speaking rapidly about a new mission that cannot wait during an outpatient visit.
Psychotic intensity or hospitalization has become part of the episode.
Energy is increased rather than simply mood being happy.
The person feels unusually powerful and says sleep is unnecessary.
A major depressive episode is common but is not required for the diagnosis.
The final clue is mania with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, risk, and marked impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar I Disorder because it describes a manic episode with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, risky behavior, and marked impairment or psychosis.

cases.title: Training Case 837
clues: A 47-year-old office worker reports pain or fatigue that has taken over daily life during an outpatient visit.
The symptoms are not intentionally produced.
The person repeatedly researches symptoms and seeks consultations.
Functional impairment comes from health-related thoughts, feelings, and behaviors.
Illness Anxiety Disorder has minimal or no somatic symptoms.
The final clue is disproportionate health-related preoccupation around actual symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Somatic Symptom Disorder because distressing physical symptoms are accompanied by excessive health-related thoughts, anxiety, and behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 838
clues: A 63-year-old retiree has daytime fatigue and irritability despite adequate chance to sleep during a family-requested evaluation.
Anxiety or depression may coexist but does not fully explain the sleep complaint.
The problem persists despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep.
Sleep effort and clock-watching worsen arousal.
Circadian rhythm disorders involve misalignment of sleep timing.
The final clue is chronic insomnia symptoms with functional consequences and no better primary sleep-wake explanation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 839
clues: A 41-year-old parent reports chronic emptiness, abandonment panic, and impulsive crises during a primary care consultation.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
Mood shifts are rapid and reactive, often lasting hours rather than discrete mood episodes.
Fear of abandonment drives frantic efforts to prevent separation.
Bipolar II mood episodes last days and include increased energy, not only interpersonal reactivity.
The final clue is abandonment fear, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, and self-harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 840
clues: A 20-year-old student describes years of alternating emotional highs and lows that never fully meet episode thresholds during a telehealth follow-up.
Symptoms are present much of the time and create relationship and work problems.
There are no psychotic symptoms and no history of hospitalization for mood elevation.
Others notice cycles of activation and discouragement.
The diagnosis depends on chronic subthreshold cycling.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a chronic bipolar-spectrum pattern below full mood episode thresholds.
cases.explanation: The case points to Cyclothymic Disorder because it describes a chronic pattern of fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full episode criteria.

cases.title: Training Case 841
clues: A 28-year-old artist has become socially withdrawn and increasingly suspicious during an outpatient visit.
Negative symptoms such as reduced speech, low motivation, or flat affect are present.
Hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech, or disorganized behavior are prominent.
The person believes others are monitoring thoughts and hears voices commenting on actions.
Schizoaffective Disorder requires a substantial mood component plus psychosis outside mood episodes.
The best-fitting diagnosis is chronic psychosis with functional decline and negative symptoms.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophrenia because psychotic symptoms, negative symptoms, functional decline, and a prolonged course occur outside mood episodes and without a better substance or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 842
clues: A 41-year-old parent presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during a student health appointment.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
The pattern is long-standing and appears across relationships and settings.
Relationships alternate between idealization and devaluation.
Bipolar II mood episodes last days and include increased energy, not only interpersonal reactivity.
The final clue is abandonment fear, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, and self-harm.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 843
clues: A 24-year-old student continues stimulant use despite chest pain, job loss, and relationship conflict during a primary care consultation.
Tolerance, risky use, and continued use despite harm may be present.
Financial, legal, occupational, or interpersonal consequences are mounting.
Attempts to quit trigger dysphoria, fatigue, and craving.
Primary psychotic disorder persists beyond substance effects.
The final clue is stimulant binges with insomnia, crash, paranoia, and loss of control.
cases.explanation: The case points to Stimulant Use Disorder because stimulant use shows impaired control, craving, risky use, role impairment, and continued use despite physical or psychological harm.

cases.title: Training Case 844
clues: A 31-year-old entrepreneur is brought in after several nights with almost no sleep and escalating plans during a telehealth follow-up.
Family reports pressured speech, grandiosity, distractibility, and reduced need for sleep.
The change has lasted long enough to disrupt work, family, and safety.
The person feels unusually powerful and says sleep is unnecessary.
The symptoms are not better explained by stimulant intoxication or a primary psychotic disorder.
The best-fitting diagnosis requires at least one manic episode.
cases.explanation: The case points to Bipolar I Disorder because it describes a manic episode with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, risky behavior, and marked impairment or psychosis.

cases.title: Training Case 845
clues: A 43-year-old neighbor seeks help because one fixed belief has dominated life for months during a student health appointment.
Substances and medical causes do not explain the belief.
The belief has persisted for at least a month.
The person acts on the belief through complaints, investigations, or confrontations.
OCD can have poor insight but thoughts are often intrusive and ritual-linked.

Weight can vary and is not the diagnostic requirement.
The person feels disgusted, depressed, or guilty afterward.
The absence of compensatory behavior separates it from bulimia.
Bulimia Nervosa includes recurrent compensatory behaviors.
The best-fitting diagnosis is recurrent distressing binge eating without compensatory behavior.
cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 946
clues: A 20-year-old musician has hallucinations and delusions but the duration remains intermediate during an outpatient visit.
There is no clear substance, medication, or medical cause.
The disturbance has lasted more than a month but less than six months.
The person may be too early in the course to determine whether schizophrenia will develop.
Schizophrenia requires a longer illness duration.
The final clue is active psychotic symptoms lasting more than a month but not yet six months.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophreniform Disorder because schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms have lasted longer than brief psychosis but have not reached the longer schizophrenia duration threshold.

cases.title: Training Case 947
clues: A 35-year-old parent dreads bedtime because sleep has become unpredictable during a primary care consultation.
The problem persists despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep.
Caffeine, shift work, substances, and untreated sleep apnea are assessed.
Poor sleep becomes a conditioned cycle.
Circadian rhythm disorders involve misalignment of sleep timing.
The best-fitting diagnosis is persistent sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty with daytime impairment despite adequate sleep opportunity.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 948
clues: A 63-year-old retiree dreads bedtime because sleep has become unpredictable during a family-requested evaluation.
The sleep difficulty occurs repeatedly over months.
Anxiety or depression may coexist but does not fully explain the sleep complaint.
Poor sleep becomes a conditioned cycle.
Circadian rhythm disorders involve misalignment of sleep timing.
The final clue is chronic insomnia symptoms with functional consequences and no better primary sleep-wake explanation.
cases.explanation: The case points to Insomnia Disorder because sleep initiation or maintenance difficulty persists despite adequate opportunity and causes daytime impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 949
clues: A 36-year-old parent has been unable to re-engage with life long after a bereavement during a telehealth follow-up.
The symptoms are culturally disproportionate in duration or severity.
The loss occurred well beyond the expected acute mourning period.

The case favors Prolonged Grief Disorder because grief-specific longing remains the central syndrome.
cases.explanation: The case points to Prolonged Grief Disorder because persistent yearning and preoccupation after bereavement remain intense, impairing, and grief-specific well beyond the acute mourning period.

cases.title: Training Case 950
clues: A 36-year-old engineer says the mind rarely turns off even when problems are minor during a family-requested evaluation.

The worry has been present for months and spans health, money, work, family, and small decisions.

The best-fitting diagnosis is chronic excessive worry across many domains with physical tension and impairment.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 951
clues: A 43-year-old mechanic is referred after rooms became unusable because of saved items during a student health appointment.

The person believes items may be needed, are sentimental, or must not be wasted.
Discarding even low-value objects causes marked distress.
Collectors organize and display items; hoarding creates clutter and impairment.
The case favors Hoarding Disorder because clutter and saving are the central syndrome.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 952
clues: A 9-year-old child is referred for chronic disorganization, forgetfulness, and restlessness during a primary care consultation.
The symptoms cause school, work, relationship, or safety impairment.
Inattention includes losing items, careless mistakes, distractibility, and difficulty finishing tasks.

Anxiety can impair concentration but is driven by worry.
The case favors ADHD because symptoms are chronic, cross-situational, and began early.

cases.title: Training Case 953
clues: A 55-year-old chef has recurrent binge episodes associated with distress during a student health appointment.

Binges occur with rapid eating, eating when not hungry, eating until uncomfortably full, or eating alone from embarrassment.

cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 954
clues: A 20-year-old musician is evaluated after recent onset of disorganized speech and suspicious beliefs during a primary care consultation.

cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophreniform Disorder because schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms have lasted longer than brief psychosis but have not reached the longer schizophrenia duration threshold.

cases.explanation: The case points to Binge-Eating Disorder because recurrent loss-of-control binge episodes cause distress without regular compensatory behaviors.

cases.title: Training Case 960
clues: A 29-year-old bartender continues drinking despite arguments, blackouts, and medical concerns during a family-requested evaluation.

The final clue is continued alcohol use despite harm plus tolerance or withdrawal.

cases.title: Training Case 961
clues: A 43-year-old nurse reports withdrawal symptoms that improve after opioid use during a telehealth follow-up.

cases.title: Training Case 963
clues: A 50-year-old train operator avoids reminders of a life-threatening experience during a telehealth follow-up.

cases.title: Training Case 964
