cases.explanation: The case points to Social Anxiety Disorder because anxiety and avoidance center on possible negative evaluation in social or performance situations, causing impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 1194
clues: A 34-year-old warehouse worker is brought in after months of odd beliefs and functional decline during an emergency assessment.
Substance use and medical causes do not explain the psychosis.
Negative symptoms such as reduced speech, low motivation, or flat affect are present.
The psychotic symptoms persist outside mood episodes.
Delusional Disorder lacks the broader deterioration and hallucination/disorganization pattern.
The final clue is psychosis lasting months with deterioration and symptoms not restricted to mood episodes.
cases.explanation: The case points to Schizophrenia because psychotic symptoms, negative symptoms, functional decline, and a prolonged course occur outside mood episodes and without a better substance or medical explanation.

cases.title: Training Case 1195
clues: A 8-year-old child has restricted eating because of sensory aversion, low interest, or fear of consequences during a primary care consultation.
Mealtimes create family stress and social avoidance.
There is no intense fear of weight gain or disturbance in body image.
Restriction can be driven by sensory sensitivity, low appetite, or fear of aversive outcomes.
Autism may coexist, but ARFID describes the feeding disturbance itself.
The case favors ARFID because eating restriction is clinically impairing but not anorexia.
cases.explanation: The case points to Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder because restrictive intake causes nutritional or psychosocial impairment without weight or shape disturbance.

cases.title: Training Case 1196
clues: A 18-year-old student presents with muscle tension, poor sleep, and difficulty controlling worry during a telehealth follow-up.
The anxiety is not limited to scrutiny, contamination, trauma reminders, or a single phobic object.
The person feels restless, fatigued, irritable, and unable to concentrate.
Avoidance and reassurance-seeking are spreading across daily life.
The main symptom is worry, not obsessions, panic surges, or traumatic re-experiencing.
The case favors a generalized anxiety pattern rather than a circumscribed fear disorder.
cases.explanation: The case points to Generalized Anxiety Disorder because worry is excessive, hard to control, broad in focus, persistent, and associated with tension, sleep disturbance, and impairment.

cases.title: Training Case 1197
clues: A 65-year-old retiree holds a fixed belief that is not shared by others but otherwise appears organized during a primary care consultation.
The belief has persisted for at least a month.
There is no broad disorganized speech, negative symptoms, or marked deterioration.
The person acts on the belief through complaints, investigations, or confrontations.
Schizophrenia has broader psychosis and functional decline.
The case favors Delusional Disorder because the psychosis is circumscribed.
cases.explanation: The case points to Delusional Disorder because a fixed delusional belief persists while broader schizophrenia-spectrum features and marked functional decline are absent.

cases.title: Training Case 1198
clues: A 50-year-old train operator avoids reminders of a life-threatening experience during a primary care consultation.
Symptoms have lasted more than a month and are impairing work or relationships.
Hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, poor sleep, and anger outbursts are present.
The symptoms are trauma-linked rather than free-floating anxiety.
Adjustment Disorder lacks the full trauma symptom clusters.
The best-fitting diagnosis is a trauma-related syndrome lasting more than a month with intrusion, avoidance, mood changes, and arousal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Posttraumatic Stress Disorder because trauma exposure is followed by persistent intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition or mood changes, hyperarousal, and impairment beyond the first month.

cases.title: Training Case 1199
clues: A 35-year-old server presents after self-injury during an interpersonal conflict during a primary care consultation.
Impulsivity may involve spending, sex, substances, driving, or binge eating.
Identity, goals, and self-image feel unstable.
Anger, emptiness, and identity disturbance are prominent.
PTSD is trauma-anchored with intrusion and avoidance clusters.
The case favors Borderline Personality Disorder because the pattern is trait-like and interpersonal.
cases.explanation: The case points to Borderline Personality Disorder because it describes a pervasive pattern of unstable relationships, self-image, affect, and impulses with abandonment fears and self-harm.

cases.title: Training Case 1200
clues: A 61-year-old retiree feels intense distress when family tries to discard possessions during an outpatient visit.
Acquiring items may be excessive but the key problem is discarding.
The person believes items may be needed, are sentimental, or must not be wasted.
Rooms cannot be used for intended purposes.
Collectors organize and display items; hoarding creates clutter and impairment.
The final clue is distress with discarding plus living areas blocked by saved items.
cases.explanation: The case points to Hoarding Disorder because persistent difficulty discarding possessions leads to cluttered living spaces, distress, and impairment.
